infobox.key: Anthony Ashley Cooper, 1st Earl of Shaftesbury
infobox.value: [PC](/wiki/Privy_Counsellor_(England) "Privy Counsellor (England)") [FRS](/wiki/Fellow_of_the_Royal_Society "Fellow of the Royal Society") (22 July 1621 – 21 January 1683), was an English statesman and peer. He held senior political office under both the [Commonwealth of England](/wiki/Commonwealth_of_England "Commonwealth of England") and [Charles II](/wiki/Charles_II_of_England "Charles II of England"), serving as [Chancellor of the Exchequer](/wiki/Chancellor_of_the_Exchequer "Chancellor of the Exchequer") from 1661 to 1672 and [Lord Chancellor](/wiki/Lord_Chancellor "Lord Chancellor") from 1672 to 1673. During the [Exclusion Crisis](/wiki/Exclusion_Crisis "Exclusion Crisis") (1679-1681), Shaftesbury headed the movement to bar the [Catholic](/wiki/Catholic_Church "Catholic Church") heir, [James II](/wiki/James_II_of_England "James II of England"), from the royal succession, which is often seen as the origin of the [Whig party](/wiki/Whig_(British_political_party) "Whig (British political party)"). He was the first patron of [John Locke](/wiki/John_Locke "John Locke"), who, when they met was a quiet Oxford don and practicing physician; as part of Shaftesbury's household and retinue for 15 years, Locke became a major [political philosopher](/wiki/Political_philosopher "Political philosopher").

**Anthony Ashley Cooper, 1st Earl of Shaftesbury** [PC](/wiki/Privy_Counsellor_(England) "Privy Counsellor (England)") [FRS](/wiki/Fellow_of_the_Royal_Society "Fellow of the Royal Society") (22 July 1621 – 21 January 1683), was an English statesman and peer. He held senior political office under both the [Commonwealth of England](/wiki/Commonwealth_of_England "Commonwealth of England") and [Charles II](/wiki/Charles_II_of_England "Charles II of England"), serving as [Chancellor of the Exchequer](/wiki/Chancellor_of_the_Exchequer "Chancellor of the Exchequer") from 1661 to 1672 and [Lord Chancellor](/wiki/Lord_Chancellor "Lord Chancellor") from 1672 to 1673. During the [Exclusion Crisis](/wiki/Exclusion_Crisis "Exclusion Crisis") (1679-1681), Shaftesbury headed the movement to bar the [Catholic](/wiki/Catholic_Church "Catholic Church") heir, [James II](/wiki/James_II_of_England "James II of England"), from the royal succession, which is often seen as the origin of the [Whig party](/wiki/Whig_(British_political_party) "Whig (British political party)"). He was the first patron of [John Locke](/wiki/John_Locke "John Locke"), who, when they met was a quiet Oxford don and practicing physician; as part of Shaftesbury's household and retinue for 15 years, Locke became a major [political philosopher](/wiki/Political_philosopher "Political philosopher").

During the [Wars of the Three Kingdoms](/wiki/Wars_of_the_Three_Kingdoms "Wars of the Three Kingdoms"), Shaftesbury initially supported the [Royalists](/wiki/Cavalier "Cavalier"), before joining the [Parliamentarians](/wiki/Roundhead "Roundhead") in 1644. He served on the [English Council of State](/wiki/English_Council_of_State "English Council of State") under the [English Commonwealth](/wiki/English_Commonwealth "English Commonwealth"), although he opposed [Oliver Cromwell](/wiki/Oliver_Cromwell "Oliver Cromwell")'s attempt to rule without Parliament during the [Rule of the Major-Generals](/wiki/Rule_of_the_Major-Generals "Rule of the Major-Generals") (1655–1657). He backed the [Stuart Restoration](/wiki/Stuart_Restoration "Stuart Restoration") in May 1660, and was raised to the [peerage of England](/wiki/Peerage_of_England "Peerage of England") as Lord Ashley by Charles II.

After the political fall in 1672 of [Edward Hyde, 1st Earl of Clarendon](/wiki/Edward_Hyde,_1st_Earl_of_Clarendon "Edward Hyde, 1st Earl of Clarendon"), Lord Ashley was created [Earl of Shaftesbury](/wiki/Earl_of_Shaftesbury "Earl of Shaftesbury") and became one of five members of the so-called [Cabal Ministry](/wiki/Cabal_Ministry "Cabal Ministry"). In 1673, it became widely known that Charles's brother and heir James had secretly converted to Catholicism. As with many English Protestants of the period, Shaftesbury saw Catholicism as closely linked to "arbitrary government", and thus the prospect of a Catholic monarch as a threat to the rule of Parliament.[[a]](#cite_note-2)

His sponsorship of the Exclusion Bill in 1679 led to two years of political struggle, but ultimately ended in defeat. During the subsequent [Tory](/wiki/Tory_(British_political_party) "Tory (British political party)") reaction in 1681, Shaftesbury was arrested for [high treason](/wiki/High_treason "High treason"), a prosecution dropped several months later. Fearing re-arrest and execution, which was the fate of other opponents of the King, in 1682 he went into exile in [Amsterdam](/wiki/Amsterdam "Amsterdam"), where he died in January 1683.

## Biography

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Anthony_Ashley_Cooper,_1st_Earl_of_Shaftesbury&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Biography")]

### Early life and first marriage, 1621–1640

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Anthony_Ashley_Cooper,_1st_Earl_of_Shaftesbury&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Early life and first marriage, 1621–1640")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/32/EnglandDorset.svg/250px-EnglandDorset.svg.png)](/wiki/File:EnglandDorset.svg)

Location of [Dorset](/wiki/Dorset "Dorset") in England. Anthony Ashley Cooper was born in Dorset in 1621, and he would maintain important links with the county throughout his political career.

Cooper was the eldest son and successor of [Sir John Cooper, 1st Baronet](/wiki/Sir_John_Cooper,_1st_Baronet "Sir John Cooper, 1st Baronet"), of [Rockbourne](/wiki/Rockbourne "Rockbourne") in Hampshire, and his mother was the former Anne Ashley, daughter and sole heiress of [Sir Anthony Ashley, 1st Baronet](/wiki/Sir_Anthony_Ashley,_1st_Baronet "Sir Anthony Ashley, 1st Baronet"). He was born on 22 July 1621, at the home of his maternal grandfather Sir Anthony Ashley in [Wimborne St Giles](/wiki/Wimborne_St_Giles "Wimborne St Giles"), Dorset.[[2]](#cite_note-ODNB-3) He was named Anthony Ashley Cooper because of a promise the couple had made to Sir Anthony.[[2]](#cite_note-ODNB-3)

Although Sir Anthony Ashley was of minor gentry stock, he had served as [Secretary at War](/wiki/Secretary_at_War "Secretary at War") in the reign of Queen [Elizabeth I](/wiki/Elizabeth_I_of_England "Elizabeth I of England") (d.1603). In 1622, two years after the death of his first wife, Sir Anthony Ashley married the 19-year-old Philippa Sheldon (51 years his junior), a relative of [George Villiers, Marquess of Buckingham](/wiki/George_Villiers,_1st_Duke_of_Buckingham "George Villiers, 1st Duke of Buckingham"), thus cementing relations with the most powerful man at court.[[2]](#cite_note-ODNB-3)

Cooper's father was created a [baronet](/wiki/Baronet "Baronet") in 1622, and he represented [Poole](/wiki/Poole_(UK_Parliament_constituency) "Poole (UK Parliament constituency)") in the parliaments of 1625 and 1628, supporting the attack on [Richard Neile](/wiki/Richard_Neile "Richard Neile"), [Bishop of Winchester](/wiki/Bishop_of_Winchester "Bishop of Winchester"), for his [Arminian](/wiki/Arminian "Arminian") tendencies. Sir Anthony Ashley insisted that a man with [Puritan](/wiki/Puritan "Puritan") leanings, Aaron Guerdon, be chosen as Cooper's first tutor.[[2]](#cite_note-ODNB-3)

Cooper's mother died in 1628. In the following year his father remarried, this time to the widowed Mary Moryson, one of the daughters of wealthy London textile merchant [Baptist Hicks](/wiki/Baptist_Hicks "Baptist Hicks") and co-[heir](/wiki/Heir "Heir") of his fortune. Through his stepmother, Cooper thus gained an important political connection in the form of her grandson, the future [1st Earl of Essex](/wiki/Arthur_Capell,_1st_Earl_of_Essex "Arthur Capell, 1st Earl of Essex"). Cooper's father died in 1630, leaving his son (age 9) a wealthy [orphan](/wiki/Orphan "Orphan").[[2]](#cite_note-ODNB-3) Upon his father's death, he inherited his father's [baronetcy](/wiki/Baronetcy "Baronetcy") and was now Sir Anthony Ashley Cooper.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

Cooper's father had held his lands in [knight-service](/wiki/Knight-service "Knight-service"), so Cooper's inheritance now came under the authority of the [Court of Wards](/wiki/Court_of_Wards_and_Liveries "Court of Wards and Liveries").[[2]](#cite_note-ODNB-3) The [trustees](/wiki/Trustees "Trustees") whom his father had appointed to administer his estate, his brother-in-law (Anthony Ashley Cooper's uncle by marriage), Edward Tooker, and his colleague from the House of Commons, Sir [Daniel Norton](/wiki/Daniel_Norton "Daniel Norton"), purchased Cooper's wardship from the king, but they remained unable to settle the estate and sell Cooper's land without permission of the Court of Wards, since at his death, Sir John Cooper had left some £35,000 in gambling debts.[[2]](#cite_note-ODNB-3) The Court of Wards ordered the sale of the best of Sir John's lands to pay his debts, with several sales commissioners picking up choice properties at £20,000 less than their market value, a circumstance which led Cooper to hate the Court of Wards as a corrupt institution.[[2]](#cite_note-ODNB-3)

Young orphaned Cooper was sent to live with his father's trustee Sir Daniel Norton in [Southwick, Hampshire](/wiki/Southwick,_Hampshire "Southwick, Hampshire") (near [Portsmouth](/wiki/Portsmouth "Portsmouth")). Norton had joined in Sir John Cooper's denunciation of Arminianism in the 1628–29 parliament, and Norton chose a man with [Puritan](/wiki/Puritan "Puritan") leanings named Fletcher as Cooper's tutor.[[2]](#cite_note-ODNB-3)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/f1/Lincoln%27s_Inn_Gate.jpg/250px-Lincoln%27s_Inn_Gate.jpg)](/wiki/File:Lincoln%27s_Inn_Gate.jpg)

The Gate of [Lincoln's Inn](/wiki/Lincoln%27s_Inn "Lincoln's Inn"). Cooper attended Lincoln's Inn, beginning in 1638 at age 17, to receive an education in the [laws of England](/wiki/Laws_of_England "Laws of England"). Throughout his political career, Cooper posed as a defender of the [rule of law](/wiki/Rule_of_law "Rule of law"), at various points in his career breaking with both [Oliver Cromwell](/wiki/Oliver_Cromwell "Oliver Cromwell") (1599–1658) and [Charles II](/wiki/Charles_II_of_England "Charles II of England") (1630–1685) when he perceived they were subverting the rule of law and introducing arbitrary government.

Cooper's trustee Sir Daniel died in 1636, and Cooper (age 15) was sent to live with his father's other trustee, [Edward Tooker](/wiki/Edward_Tooker "Edward Tooker"), at [Maddington](/wiki/Maddington,_Wiltshire "Maddington, Wiltshire"), near Salisbury. Here his tutor was a man with an MA from [Oriel College, Oxford](/wiki/Oriel_College,_Oxford "Oriel College, Oxford").[[2]](#cite_note-ODNB-3)

Cooper matriculated at [Exeter College, Oxford](/wiki/Exeter_College,_Oxford "Exeter College, Oxford"), on 24 March 1637, aged 15,[[3]](#cite_note-4) where he studied under its master, the [Regius Professor of Divinity](/wiki/Regius_Professor_of_Divinity "Regius Professor of Divinity"), [John Prideaux](/wiki/John_Prideaux "John Prideaux"), a [Calvinist](/wiki/Calvinist "Calvinist") with vehemently anti-Arminian tendencies.[[2]](#cite_note-ODNB-3) While there, he fomented a minor riot and left without taking a degree.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] In February 1638, Cooper was admitted to [Lincoln's Inn](/wiki/Lincoln%27s_Inn "Lincoln's Inn"),[[4]](#cite_note-5)[[5]](#cite_note-HOP-6) where he was exposed to the Puritan preaching of chaplains [Edward Reynolds](/wiki/Edward_Reynolds "Edward Reynolds") and [Joseph Caryl](/wiki/Joseph_Caryl "Joseph Caryl").[[2]](#cite_note-ODNB-3)

On 25 February 1639, aged 19, Cooper married Margaret Coventry, daughter of [Thomas Coventry, 1st Baron Coventry](/wiki/Thomas_Coventry,_1st_Baron_Coventry "Thomas Coventry, 1st Baron Coventry"), who was then serving as [Lord Keeper of the Great Seal](/wiki/Lord_Keeper_of_the_Great_Seal "Lord Keeper of the Great Seal") for [Charles I](/wiki/Charles_I_of_England "Charles I of England"). As Cooper was still a [minor](/wiki/Minor_(law) "Minor (law)"), the young couple moved into Lord Coventry's residences of [Durham House](/wiki/Durham_House_(London) "Durham House (London)") in [the Strand](/wiki/Strand,_London "Strand, London"), and at [Canonbury House](/wiki/Canonbury_House_and_Canonbury_Tower "Canonbury House and Canonbury Tower") in [Islington](/wiki/Islington "Islington").[[2]](#cite_note-ODNB-3)

### Early political career, 1640–1660

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Anthony_Ashley_Cooper,_1st_Earl_of_Shaftesbury&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Early political career, 1640–1660")]

#### Parliament, 1640–1642

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Anthony_Ashley_Cooper,_1st_Earl_of_Shaftesbury&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Parliament, 1640–1642")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/9e/1stLordCoventry.jpg/250px-1stLordCoventry.jpg)](/wiki/File:1stLordCoventry.jpg)

Cooper's father-in-law [Thomas Coventry, 1st Baron Coventry](/wiki/Thomas_Coventry,_1st_Baron_Coventry "Thomas Coventry, 1st Baron Coventry") (1578–1640), who served as [Lord Keeper of the Great Seal](/wiki/Lord_Keeper_of_the_Great_Seal "Lord Keeper of the Great Seal") from 1625 to 1640. Cooper first entered politics under Lord Coventry's tutelage.

In March 1640, while still a minor, Cooper was elected Member of Parliament for the borough of [Tewkesbury](/wiki/Tewkesbury_(UK_Parliament_constituency) "Tewkesbury (UK Parliament constituency)"), Gloucestershire, in the [Short Parliament](/wiki/Short_Parliament "Short Parliament")[[5]](#cite_note-HOP-6) through the influence of Lord Coventry.[[2]](#cite_note-ODNB-3)

In October 1640, with opinion in the country swinging against the king's supporters (including Coventry), Cooper was not asked to stand for election for Tewkesbury in the Long Parliament.[[2]](#cite_note-ODNB-3) He contested, and by some accounts, won a by-election to the seat of [Downton](/wiki/Downton_(UK_Parliament_constituency) "Downton (UK Parliament constituency)") in Wiltshire, but [Denzil Holles](/wiki/Denzil_Holles,_1st_Baron_Holles "Denzil Holles, 1st Baron Holles"), soon to rise to prominence as a leader of the opposition to the King and a personal rival of Sir Anthony, blocked Cooper's admission to the Parliament.[[2]](#cite_note-ODNB-3) It was probably feared that Sir Anthony, as a result of his recent marriage to the daughter of the king's Lord Keeper, would be too sympathetic to the king.[[2]](#cite_note-ODNB-3)

#### Royalist, 1642–1644

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Anthony_Ashley_Cooper,_1st_Earl_of_Shaftesbury&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Royalist, 1642–1644")]

When the [Civil War](/wiki/English_Civil_War "English Civil War") began in 1642, Cooper initially supported the King (somewhat echoing Holles' concerns).[*[further explanation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Please_clarify "Wikipedia:Please clarify")*] After a period of vacillating, he, at his own expense in the summer of 1643, raised a regiment of [foot](/wiki/Footsoldier "Footsoldier") and a [troop](/wiki/Troop "Troop") of [horse](/wiki/Cavalry "Cavalry") for the King, serving as their [colonel](/wiki/Colonel "Colonel") and [captain](/wiki/Captain_(land) "Captain (land)") respectively.[[2]](#cite_note-ODNB-3) Following the [Royalist](/wiki/Cavalier "Cavalier") victory at the [Battle of Roundway Down](/wiki/Battle_of_Roundway_Down "Battle of Roundway Down") on 13 July 1643, Cooper was one of three commissioners appointed to negotiate the surrender of [Dorchester](/wiki/Dorchester,_Dorset "Dorchester, Dorset"), at which he negotiated a deal whereby the town agreed to surrender in exchange for being spared plunder and punishment. However, troops under [Prince Maurice](/wiki/Maurice_of_the_Palatinate "Maurice of the Palatinate") soon arrived and plundered Dorchester and [Weymouth](/wiki/Weymouth,_Dorset "Weymouth, Dorset") anyway, leading to heated words between Cooper and Prince Maurice.[[2]](#cite_note-ODNB-3)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/3a/Maurice_of_the_Palatinate.jpg/250px-Maurice_of_the_Palatinate.jpg)](/wiki/File:Maurice_of_the_Palatinate.jpg)

[Maurice of the Palatinate](/wiki/Maurice_of_the_Palatinate "Maurice of the Palatinate") (1620–1652), depicted as [Mercury](/wiki/Mercury_(mythology) "Mercury (mythology)"). Prince Maurice attempted to block Cooper's appointment as governor of [Weymouth and Portland](/wiki/Weymouth_and_Portland "Weymouth and Portland").

[William Seymour, Marquess of Hertford](/wiki/William_Seymour,_2nd_Duke_of_Somerset "William Seymour, 2nd Duke of Somerset"), the commander of the Royalist forces in the west, had recommended Cooper be appointed governor of [Weymouth and Portland](/wiki/Weymouth_and_Portland "Weymouth and Portland"), but Prince Maurice intervened to block the appointment, on grounds of Cooper's youth and alleged inexperience.[[2]](#cite_note-ODNB-3) Cooper appealed to the [Chancellor of the Exchequer](/wiki/Chancellor_of_the_Exchequer "Chancellor of the Exchequer"), [Edward Hyde](/wiki/Edward_Hyde,_1st_Earl_of_Clarendon "Edward Hyde, 1st Earl of Clarendon"); Hyde arranged a compromise whereby Cooper would be appointed as governor but resign as soon as it was possible to do so without losing face.[[2]](#cite_note-ODNB-3) Cooper was promised that upon resigning as governor, he would be made [High Sheriff of Dorset](/wiki/High_Sheriff_of_Dorset "High Sheriff of Dorset") and president of the [council of war](/wiki/Council_of_war "Council of war") for Dorset, both of which were offices more prestigious than the governorship. Cooper spent the remainder of 1643 as governor of Weymouth and Portland.[[2]](#cite_note-ODNB-3)

#### Parliamentarian and second marriage, 1644–1652

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Anthony_Ashley_Cooper,_1st_Earl_of_Shaftesbury&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Parliamentarian and second marriage, 1644–1652")]

In early 1644, Cooper resigned all of his posts under the king, and travelled to [Hurst Castle](/wiki/Hurst_Castle "Hurst Castle"), the headquarters of the [Parliamentarians](/wiki/Roundhead "Roundhead").[[2]](#cite_note-ODNB-3) Called before the [Committee of Both Kingdoms](/wiki/Committee_of_Both_Kingdoms "Committee of Both Kingdoms"), on 6 March 1644, he explained that he believed that Charles I was now being influenced by Roman Catholics. Catholics were increasingly prominent at Charles's court, and he had recently signed a truce with [Irish Catholic](/wiki/Catholic_Church_in_Ireland "Catholic Church in Ireland") rebels) and that he believed Charles had no intention of "promoting or preserving ... the Protestant religion and the liberties of the kingdom" and that he, therefore, believed the parliamentary cause was just, and he offered to take the [Solemn League and Covenant](/wiki/Solemn_League_and_Covenant "Solemn League and Covenant").[[2]](#cite_note-ODNB-3)

In July 1644, the [House of Commons](/wiki/House_of_Commons_of_England "House of Commons of England") gave Cooper permission to leave London, and he soon joined parliamentary forces in Dorset.[[2]](#cite_note-ODNB-3) After he participated in a campaign in August, parliament appointed him to the committee governing the army in Dorset.[[2]](#cite_note-ODNB-3) Cooper participated in fighting throughout 1644.[[2]](#cite_note-ODNB-3) In 1645, with the passing of the [Self-denying Ordinance](/wiki/Self-denying_Ordinance "Self-denying Ordinance"), Cooper chose to resign his commissions in the parliamentary army, which was, at this point, being supplanted by the creation of the [New Model Army](/wiki/New_Model_Army "New Model Army")) to preserve his claim to be the rightful member for Downton.[[2]](#cite_note-ODNB-3) He nevertheless continued to be active in the Dorset committee as a civil member.[[2]](#cite_note-ODNB-3)

In this period Cooper first expressed an interest in overseas [plantations](/wiki/Plantation "Plantation"), investing in a plantation in the [English colony](/wiki/British_Empire "British Empire") of [Barbados](/wiki/Barbados "Barbados") in 1646.[[2]](#cite_note-ODNB-3)[[6]](#cite_note-7)

Little is known of Cooper's activities in the late 1640s. It is often assumed that he supported the [Presbyterians](/wiki/Presbyterian "Presbyterian") against the [Independents](/wiki/Independent_(religion) "Independent (religion)"), and, as such, opposed the [regicide of Charles I](/wiki/Regicide#Execution_of_Charles_I_of_England "Regicide") in January 1649.[[2]](#cite_note-ODNB-3) Nevertheless, he was willing to work with the new regime, accepting a commission as [justice of the peace](/wiki/Justice_of_the_peace "Justice of the peace") for [Wiltshire](/wiki/Wiltshire "Wiltshire") and [Dorset](/wiki/Dorset "Dorset") in February 1649 and acting as [High Sheriff of Wiltshire](/wiki/High_Sheriff_of_Wiltshire "High Sheriff of Wiltshire") for 1647.[[2]](#cite_note-ODNB-3) In February 1650, he not only took the oath of loyalty to the new regime but was a member of a commission that tendered the oath.[[2]](#cite_note-ODNB-3)

Cooper's first wife, Margaret, died on 10 July 1649; the couple had had no children.[[2]](#cite_note-ODNB-3) Less than a year later, on 15 April 1650, Cooper remarried at age 28, to seventeen-year-old Lady Frances Cecil (1633–1652), daughter of [David Cecil, 3rd Earl of Exeter](/wiki/David_Cecil,_3rd_Earl_of_Exeter "David Cecil, 3rd Earl of Exeter").[[2]](#cite_note-ODNB-3) In their brief marriage, the couple had two children, one of whom, [Anthony](/wiki/Anthony_Ashley-Cooper,_2nd_Earl_of_Shaftesbury "Anthony Ashley-Cooper, 2nd Earl of Shaftesbury"), lived to adulthood.[[2]](#cite_note-ODNB-3) Lady Frances died on 31 December 1652, aged only 19.[[2]](#cite_note-ODNB-3)

### Statesman under the Commonwealth of England and the Protectorate, 1652–1660

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Anthony_Ashley_Cooper,_1st_Earl_of_Shaftesbury&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Statesman under the Commonwealth of England and the Protectorate, 1652–1660")]

On 17 January 1652, the [Rump Parliament](/wiki/Rump_Parliament "Rump Parliament") appointed Cooper to the committee on law reform chaired by Sir [Matthew Hale](/wiki/Matthew_Hale_(jurist) "Matthew Hale (jurist)") (the so-called Hale Commission, none of whose moderate proposals were ever enacted).[[2]](#cite_note-ODNB-3)

In March 1653, the Rump issued a full [pardon](/wiki/Pardon "Pardon") for his time as a Royalist, opening the way for his return to public office. Following the dissolution of the Rump in April 1653, [Oliver Cromwell](/wiki/Oliver_Cromwell "Oliver Cromwell") and the [Army Council](/wiki/Army_Council_(1647) "Army Council (1647)") nominated Cooper to serve in [Barebone's Parliament](/wiki/Barebone%27s_Parliament "Barebone's Parliament") as member for [Wiltshire](/wiki/Wiltshire_(UK_Parliament_constituency) "Wiltshire (UK Parliament constituency)").[[5]](#cite_note-HOP-6) On 14 July, Cromwell appointed Cooper to the [English Council of State](/wiki/English_Council_of_State "English Council of State"), where he was a member of the Committee for the Business of the Law, which was intended to continue the reform work of the Hale Commission.[[2]](#cite_note-ODNB-3) Cooper aligned himself with the moderates in Barebone's Parliament, voting against the abolition of [tithes](/wiki/Tithes "Tithes").[[2]](#cite_note-ODNB-3) He was one of the members who voted to dissolve Barebone's Parliament on 12 December 1653 rather than acquiesce to the abolition of tithes.[[2]](#cite_note-ODNB-3)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/2e/Stonehenge_-_Wiltonia_sive_Comitatus_Wiltoniensis%3B_Anglice_Wilshire_%28Atlas_van_Loon%29.jpg/250px-Stonehenge_-_Wiltonia_sive_Comitatus_Wiltoniensis%3B_Anglice_Wilshire_%28Atlas_van_Loon%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Stonehenge_-_Wiltonia_sive_Comitatus_Wiltoniensis;_Anglice_Wilshire_(Atlas_van_Loon).jpg)

Depiction of [Stonehenge](/wiki/Stonehenge "Stonehenge") in the Atlas van Loon (1649). So many voters turned up for the [Wiltshire](/wiki/Wiltshire_(UK_Parliament_constituency) "Wiltshire (UK Parliament constituency)") election in 1654, that the poll had to be switched from [Wilton](/wiki/Wilton,_Wiltshire "Wilton, Wiltshire") to Stonehenge. Cooper won the election.

When the [Instrument of Government](/wiki/Instrument_of_Government "Instrument of Government") gave England a written constitution, for the first and only time, 4 days later, Cooper was again named to the Council of State.[[2]](#cite_note-ODNB-3) During the elections for the [First Protectorate Parliament](/wiki/First_Protectorate_Parliament "First Protectorate Parliament") in summer 1654, Cooper headed a slate of ten candidates who stood in [Wiltshire](/wiki/Wiltshire_(UK_Parliament_constituency) "Wiltshire (UK Parliament constituency)") against 10 republican MPs headed by [Edmund Ludlow](/wiki/Edmund_Ludlow "Edmund Ludlow").[[2]](#cite_note-ODNB-3) On the day of the election, so many voters turned up that the poll had to be switched from [Wilton](/wiki/Wilton,_Wiltshire "Wilton, Wiltshire") to [Stonehenge](/wiki/Stonehenge "Stonehenge").[[2]](#cite_note-ODNB-3) Cooper's slate of candidates prevailed, although Ludlow alleged his party was in the majority. At the same election, Cooper was also elected MP for [Tewkesbury](/wiki/Tewkesbury_(UK_Parliament_constituency) "Tewkesbury (UK Parliament constituency)") and [Poole](/wiki/Poole_(UK_Parliament_constituency) "Poole (UK Parliament constituency)")[[5]](#cite_note-HOP-6) but chose to sit for Wiltshire. Although Cooper was generally supportive of Cromwell during the First Protectorate Parliament (he voted in favour of making Cromwell king in December 1654), he grew worried that Cromwell was increasingly inclined to rule through the Army rather than through Parliament.[[2]](#cite_note-ODNB-3) This led Cooper to break with Cromwell: in early January 1655, he stopped attending Council and introduced a resolution in parliament making it illegal to collect or pay revenue not authorised by parliament. Cromwell dissolved this parliament on 22 January 1655.[[2]](#cite_note-ODNB-3)

The exiled [Charles II](/wiki/Charles_II_of_England "Charles II of England"), hearing of Cooper's break with Cromwell, wrote to Cooper saying that he would pardon Cooper for fighting against the Crown if he would now help to bring about a restoration of the monarchy.[[2]](#cite_note-ODNB-3) Cooper did not respond, nor did he participate in the [Penruddock uprising](/wiki/Penruddock_uprising "Penruddock uprising") in March 1655.[[2]](#cite_note-ODNB-3)

On 30 August 1655, Cooper married his third wife, Margaret Spencer (1627–1693), daughter of [William Spencer, 2nd Baron Spencer of Wormleighton](/wiki/William_Spencer,_2nd_Baron_Spencer_of_Wormleighton "William Spencer, 2nd Baron Spencer of Wormleighton"), and sister of [Henry Spencer, 1st Earl of Sunderland](/wiki/Henry_Spencer,_1st_Earl_of_Sunderland "Henry Spencer, 1st Earl of Sunderland").[[2]](#cite_note-ODNB-3)

Cooper was again elected as a member for Wiltshire in the [Second Protectorate Parliament](/wiki/Second_Protectorate_Parliament "Second Protectorate Parliament"),[[5]](#cite_note-HOP-6) although when the parliament met on 17 September 1656, Cooper was one of 100 members whom the Council of State excluded from the parliament.[[2]](#cite_note-ODNB-3) Cooper was one of 65 excluded members to sign a petition protesting their exclusion that was delivered by [Sir George Booth](/wiki/George_Booth,_1st_Baron_Delamer "George Booth, 1st Baron Delamer").[[2]](#cite_note-ODNB-3) Cooper eventually took his seat in the parliament on 20 January 1658, after Cromwell accepted an amended version of the [Humble Petition and Advice](/wiki/Humble_Petition_and_Advice "Humble Petition and Advice") that stipulated that the excluded members could return to parliament. Upon his return to the house, Cooper spoke out against [Cromwell's Other House](/wiki/Cromwell%27s_Other_House "Cromwell's Other House").[[2]](#cite_note-ODNB-3)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a7/Anthony_Ashley_Cooper.jpg/250px-Anthony_Ashley_Cooper.jpg)](/wiki/File:Anthony_Ashley_Cooper.jpg)

Portrait miniature of Sir Anthony Ashley Cooper by [Samuel Cooper](/wiki/Samuel_Cooper_(painter) "Samuel Cooper (painter)")

After the death of Oliver Cromwell in September 1658, Cooper was elected to the [Third Protectorate Parliament](/wiki/Third_Protectorate_Parliament "Third Protectorate Parliament") in early 1659 as member for Wiltshire.[[5]](#cite_note-HOP-6) During the debates in this parliament, Cooper sided with the republicans who opposed the Humble Petition and Advice and insisted that the bill recognising Cromwell's son [Richard Cromwell](/wiki/Richard_Cromwell "Richard Cromwell") as [Protector](/wiki/Lord_Protector#Cromwellian_Commonwealth "Lord Protector") should limit his control over the [militia](/wiki/Militia "Militia") and eliminate the protector's ability to veto legislation.[[2]](#cite_note-ODNB-3) Cooper again spoke out against the Other House (consisting of new lords), and in favour of restoring the old [House of Lords](/wiki/House_of_Lords "House of Lords").[[2]](#cite_note-ODNB-3)

When Richard Cromwell dissolved parliament on 22 April 1659 and recalled the Rump Parliament (dissolved by Oliver Cromwell in 1653), Cooper attempted to revive his claim to sit as member for Downton. He was also re-appointed to the Council of State at this time.[[2]](#cite_note-ODNB-3) Throughout this period, many accused Cooper of harbouring royalist sympathies, but Cooper denied this.[[2]](#cite_note-ODNB-3) In August 1659, Cooper was arrested for complicity in [Sir George Booth](/wiki/George_Booth,_1st_Baron_Delamer "George Booth, 1st Baron Delamer")'s Presbyterian royalist uprising in [Cheshire](/wiki/Cheshire "Cheshire"), but in September the Council found him not guilty of any involvement.[[2]](#cite_note-ODNB-3)

In October 1659, the [New Model Army](/wiki/New_Model_Army "New Model Army") dissolved the Rump Parliament and replaced the Council of State with its own [Committee of Safety](/wiki/English_Committee_of_Safety "English Committee of Safety").[[2]](#cite_note-ODNB-3) Cooper, republicans [Sir Arthur Haselrig](/wiki/Arthur_Haselrig "Arthur Haselrig") and [Henry Neville](/wiki/Henry_Neville_(writer) "Henry Neville (writer)") and six other members of the Council of State continued to meet in secret, referring to themselves as the rightful Council of State.[[2]](#cite_note-ODNB-3) This secret Council of State came to see [Sir George Monck](/wiki/George_Monck,_1st_Duke_of_Albemarle "George Monck, 1st Duke of Albemarle"), commander of the forces in Scotland, as the best hope to restore the Rump. Cooper and Haselrig met with Monck's commissioners, urging them to restore the Rump. Cooper was involved in several plots to launch pro-Rump uprisings.[[2]](#cite_note-ODNB-3) This proved unnecessary as, on 23 December 1659, troops resolved to stand by the Rump and the Council of State and disobey the Committee of Safety.[[2]](#cite_note-ODNB-3) The Rump Parliament reassembled on 26 December 1659, and on 2 January 1660, Cooper was elected to the Council of State.[[2]](#cite_note-ODNB-3) On 7 January 1659, a special committee reported back on the disputed 1640 Downton election and Cooper was finally allowed to take his seat as member for Downton.[[2]](#cite_note-ODNB-3)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/1a/Sir_George_Monck.jpg/250px-Sir_George_Monck.jpg)](/wiki/File:Sir_George_Monck.jpg)

[Sir George Monck](/wiki/George_Monck,_1st_Duke_of_Albemarle "George Monck, 1st Duke of Albemarle") (1608–1670). In the complicated politics of 1659, Cooper was in contact with Monck, encouraging him to march on London and then to recall the [Long Parliament](/wiki/Long_Parliament "Long Parliament"), and ultimately [restore](/wiki/English_Restoration "English Restoration") the English monarchy.

Upon General Monck's march into London, Monck was displeased that the Rump Parliament was not prepared to confirm him as commander-in-chief of the army.[[2]](#cite_note-ODNB-3) At Cooper's urging, Monck's troops marched into London, and Monck sent parliament a letter insisting that the vacant seats in the Rump Parliament be filled by by-elections.[[2]](#cite_note-ODNB-3) When the Rump insisted on placing restrictions on who could stand in these by-elections, Cooper urged Monck to insist instead on the return of the members of the Long Parliament excluded by [Pride's Purge](/wiki/Pride%27s_Purge "Pride's Purge"), and Monck obliged on 21 February 1660.[[2]](#cite_note-ODNB-3) Two days later, the restored Long Parliament again elected Cooper to the Council of State. On 16 March 1660, the Long Parliament finally voted for its own dissolution.[[5]](#cite_note-HOP-6)

Beginning in spring 1660, Cooper drew closer to the royalist cause. As late as mid-April, he appears to have favoured only a conditional restoration. In April 1660 he was re-elected MP for Wiltshire in the [Convention Parliament](/wiki/Convention_Parliament_(1660) "Convention Parliament (1660)").[[5]](#cite_note-HOP-6) On 25 April he voted in favour of an unconditional restoration.[[2]](#cite_note-ODNB-3) On 8 May, the Convention Parliament appointed Cooper as one of twelve members to travel to [The Hague](/wiki/The_Hague "The Hague") to invite Charles II to return to England.[[2]](#cite_note-ODNB-3)

### Restoration politician, 1660–1683

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Anthony_Ashley_Cooper,_1st_Earl_of_Shaftesbury&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Restoration politician, 1660–1683")]

Cooper returned to England with Charles in late May.[[2]](#cite_note-ODNB-3) On the recommendation of General Monck and of Cooper's wife's uncle [Thomas Wriothesley, 4th Earl of Southampton](/wiki/Thomas_Wriothesley,_4th_Earl_of_Southampton "Thomas Wriothesley, 4th Earl of Southampton"), Charles appointed Cooper to his [Privy Council](/wiki/Her_Majesty%27s_Most_Honourable_Privy_Council "Her Majesty's Most Honourable Privy Council") on 27 May 1660.[[2]](#cite_note-ODNB-3) Cooper took advantage of the [Declaration of Breda](/wiki/Declaration_of_Breda "Declaration of Breda") and was formally pardoned for his support of the English Commonwealth on 27 June 1660.[[2]](#cite_note-ODNB-3) During this period, he helped reorganise the Privy Council's [committee on trade and plantations](/wiki/Board_of_Trade "Board of Trade").[[2]](#cite_note-ODNB-3)

Cooper thus became a spokesman for the government in the Convention Parliament.[[2]](#cite_note-ODNB-3) However, during the debates on the [Indemnity and Oblivion Bill](/wiki/Indemnity_and_Oblivion_Act "Indemnity and Oblivion Act"), Cooper urged leniency for those who had sided with Parliament during the English Civil Wars or collaborated with the Cromwellian regime.[[2]](#cite_note-ODNB-3) He argued that only [those individuals who had personal involvement in the decision to execute Charles I](/wiki/List_of_regicides_of_Charles_I "List of regicides of Charles I") by participating in [his trial](/wiki/High_Court_of_Justice_for_the_trial_of_Charles_I "High Court of Justice for the trial of Charles I") and [execution](/wiki/Regicide#Execution_of_Charles_I_of_England "Regicide") should be exempt from the general pardon.[[2]](#cite_note-ODNB-3) This view prevailed. After the Indemnity and Oblivion Act became law on 29 August 1660, Cooper sat on the special commission that tried the regicides, and in this capacity took part in sentencing to death several colleagues with whom he had collaborated during the years of the [English Interregnum](/wiki/English_Interregnum "English Interregnum"), including [Hugh Peters](/wiki/Hugh_Peters "Hugh Peters"), [Thomas Harrison](/wiki/Thomas_Harrison_(soldier) "Thomas Harrison (soldier)"), and [Thomas Scot](/wiki/Thomas_Scot "Thomas Scot").[[2]](#cite_note-ODNB-3) As a long-time foe of the Court of Wards, during the debate on the [Tenures Abolition Bill](/wiki/Tenures_Abolition_Act_1660 "Tenures Abolition Act 1660"), Cooper supported continuing the [excise](/wiki/Excise "Excise") imposed by the Long Parliament to compensate the crown for the loss of revenues associated with the abolition of the court.[[2]](#cite_note-ODNB-3)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/31/Charles_II_of_England_in_Coronation_robes.jpg/250px-Charles_II_of_England_in_Coronation_robes.jpg)](/wiki/File:Charles_II_of_England_in_Coronation_robes.jpg)

[Charles II](/wiki/Charles_II_of_England "Charles II of England") (1630–1685) in his coronation robes, 1661. Cooper was one of twelve members of Parliament who travelled to the Dutch Republic to invite Charles to return to England, and in 1661, Charles created Cooper Lord Ashley.

On 20 April 1661, three days before his [coronation](/wiki/Coronation_of_the_English_monarch "Coronation of the English monarch") at [Westminster Abbey](/wiki/Westminster_Abbey "Westminster Abbey"), Charles II announced his coronation honours, and in those honours, he created Cooper **Baron Ashley**, of Wimborne St Giles.[[2]](#cite_note-ODNB-3)

#### Chancellor of the Exchequer, 1661–1672

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Anthony_Ashley_Cooper,_1st_Earl_of_Shaftesbury&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Chancellor of the Exchequer, 1661–1672")]

Following the coronation, the [Cavalier Parliament](/wiki/Cavalier_Parliament "Cavalier Parliament") met beginning on 8 May 1661. Lord Ashley took his seat in the [House of Lords](/wiki/House_of_Lords "House of Lords") on 11 May.[[2]](#cite_note-ODNB-3) On 11 May, the king appointed Ashley as his [Chancellor of the Exchequer](/wiki/Chancellor_of_the_Exchequer "Chancellor of the Exchequer") and under-treasurer (Southampton, Ashley's uncle by marriage, was at the time [Lord High Treasurer](/wiki/Lord_High_Treasurer "Lord High Treasurer")).[[2]](#cite_note-ODNB-3)

In 1661–1662, Ashley opposed Charles's marriage to [Catherine of Braganza](/wiki/Catherine_of_Braganza "Catherine of Braganza"), a Catholic, because the marriage would involve supporting the [Portuguese](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Portugal "Kingdom of Portugal"), and Portugal's ally [France](/wiki/Kingdom_of_France "Kingdom of France"), in Portugal's struggle against [Spain](/wiki/Habsburg_Spain "Habsburg Spain").[[2]](#cite_note-ODNB-3) Ashley was opposed to a policy that moved England into the French orbit.[[2]](#cite_note-ODNB-3) During this debate, Ashley opposed the policy engineered by Charles's [Lord Chancellor](/wiki/Lord_Chancellor "Lord Chancellor"), the [Earl of Clarendon](/wiki/Edward_Hyde,_1st_Earl_of_Clarendon "Edward Hyde, 1st Earl of Clarendon"), thus beginning what would prove to be a long-running political rivalry with Clarendon.[[2]](#cite_note-ODNB-3)

When the Cavalier Parliament set about enacting the [Clarendon Code](/wiki/Clarendon_Code "Clarendon Code"), Ashley supported a policy of moderation towards [Protestant](/wiki/Protestant "Protestant") dissenters.[[2]](#cite_note-ODNB-3) In July 1662, Ashley sponsored an amendment to the [Act of Uniformity](/wiki/Act_of_Uniformity_1662 "Act of Uniformity 1662") that would have allowed Protestant [Nonconformists](/wiki/Nonconformist_(Protestantism) "Nonconformist (Protestantism)") to allow for late subscription, giving moderate dissenters an additional opportunity to conform. In the latter half of 1662, Ashley joined Sir [Henry Bennet](/wiki/Henry_Bennet,_1st_Earl_of_Arlington "Henry Bennet, 1st Earl of Arlington"), [the Earl of Bristol](/wiki/George_Digby,_2nd_Earl_of_Bristol "George Digby, 2nd Earl of Bristol"), and [Lord Robartes](/wiki/John_Robartes,_1st_Earl_of_Radnor "John Robartes, 1st Earl of Radnor") in urging Charles to dispense peaceable Protestant Nonconformists and loyal Catholics from the Act of Uniformity.[[2]](#cite_note-ODNB-3) This led to Charles issuing his first Declaration of Indulgence on 26 December 1662.[[2]](#cite_note-ODNB-3) The Cavalier Parliament forced Charles to withdraw this declaration in February 1663.[[2]](#cite_note-ODNB-3) Ashley then supported Lord Robartes' Dispensing Bill, which would have dispensed Protestant Nonconformists, but not Catholics, from the Act of Uniformity.[[2]](#cite_note-ODNB-3) During the debate on the Dispensing Bill in the House of Lords, Ashley criticised [Edward Hyde, 1st Earl of Clarendon](/wiki/Edward_Hyde,_1st_Earl_of_Clarendon "Edward Hyde, 1st Earl of Clarendon"), Charles's [Lord Chancellor](/wiki/Lord_Chancellor "Lord Chancellor"), for opposing the [royal prerogative](/wiki/Royal_prerogative "Royal prerogative") to dispense with laws. Clarendon remarked that in his opinion, the declaration was "[Ship-Money](/wiki/Ship_money "Ship money") in religion".[[2]](#cite_note-ODNB-3) The king looked favourably on Ashley's remarks and was displeased by Clarendon's.[[2]](#cite_note-ODNB-3)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/28/Peter_Lely_%281618-1680%29_%28after%29_-_Sir_Edward_Hyde_%281609%E2%80%931674%29%2C_1st_Earl_of_Clarendon_-_1257076_-_National_Trust.jpg/250px-Peter_Lely_%281618-1680%29_%28after%29_-_Sir_Edward_Hyde_%281609%E2%80%931674%29%2C_1st_Earl_of_Clarendon_-_1257076_-_National_Trust.jpg)](/wiki/File:Peter_Lely_(1618-1680)_(after)_-_Sir_Edward_Hyde_(1609%E2%80%931674),_1st_Earl_of_Clarendon_-_1257076_-_National_Trust.jpg)

[Edward Hyde, 1st Earl of Clarendon](/wiki/Edward_Hyde,_1st_Earl_of_Clarendon "Edward Hyde, 1st Earl of Clarendon") (1609–1674), Charles II's [Lord Chancellor](/wiki/Lord_Chancellor "Lord Chancellor") from 1658 to 1667. Ashley clashed with Clarendon throughout the 1660s, but Ashley refused to support the impeachment of Clarendon in 1667.

In May 1663, Ashley was one of eight [Lords Proprietors](/wiki/Lords_Proprietors "Lords Proprietors") (Lord Clarendon was one of the others) given title to a huge tract of land in North America, which eventually became the [Province of Carolina](/wiki/Province_of_Carolina "Province of Carolina"), named in honour of King Charles.[[2]](#cite_note-ODNB-3) Later, Ashley and his assistant [John Locke](/wiki/John_Locke "John Locke"), whom he met in Oxford in 1666, drafted a plan for the colony known as the [Grand Model](/wiki/Grand_Model_for_the_Province_of_Carolina "Grand Model for the Province of Carolina"), which included the [Fundamental Constitutions of Carolina](/wiki/Fundamental_Constitutions_of_Carolina "Fundamental Constitutions of Carolina") and a framework for settlement and development.

By early 1664, Ashley was a member of the circle of [John Maitland, 1st Duke of Lauderdale](/wiki/John_Maitland,_1st_Duke_of_Lauderdale "John Maitland, 1st Duke of Lauderdale"), who ranged themselves in opposition to Lord Clarendon.

During the debate on the [Conventicle Bill](/wiki/Conventicle_Act_1664 "Conventicle Act 1664") in May 1664, Ashley proposed mitigating the harshness of the penalties initially suggested by the House of Commons.[[2]](#cite_note-ODNB-3)

Throughout late 1664 and 1665, Ashley was increasingly in the royal favour.[[2]](#cite_note-ODNB-3) For example, in August 1665, the king paid a surprise visit to Ashley at Wimborne St Giles, and, during a later visit, introduced Ashley to his [illegitimate](/wiki/Illegitimate "Illegitimate") son [James Scott, 1st Duke of Monmouth](/wiki/James_Scott,_1st_Duke_of_Monmouth "James Scott, 1st Duke of Monmouth").[[2]](#cite_note-ODNB-3)

The [Second Anglo–Dutch War](/wiki/Second_Anglo%E2%80%93Dutch_War "Second Anglo–Dutch War") began on 4 March 1665 after a [Royal Navy](/wiki/Royal_Navy "Royal Navy") squadron seized the [Dutch colony](/wiki/Dutch_Empire "Dutch Empire") of [New Netherland](/wiki/New_Netherland "New Netherland").[[2]](#cite_note-ODNB-3) During the parliamentary session of October 1665, Sir [George Downing](/wiki/Sir_George_Downing,_1st_Baronet "Sir George Downing, 1st Baronet") proposed that the use of funds voted to the crown should be restricted to the sole purpose of carrying on the war.[[2]](#cite_note-ODNB-3) Ashley opposed this proposal on the grounds that crown ministers should have flexibility in deciding how to use money received from parliamentary taxation.[[2]](#cite_note-ODNB-3)

During the 1666–1667 parliamentary session Ashley supported the Irish Cattle Bill, introduced by [the Duke of Buckingham](/wiki/George_Villiers,_2nd_Duke_of_Buckingham "George Villiers, 2nd Duke of Buckingham"), which sought to prevent the importation of Irish cattle into England.[[2]](#cite_note-ODNB-3) During the course of this debate, Ashley attacked Charles's [Lord Lieutenant of Ireland](/wiki/Lord_Lieutenant_of_Ireland "Lord Lieutenant of Ireland"), [James Butler, 1st Duke of Ormonde](/wiki/James_Butler,_1st_Duke_of_Ormonde "James Butler, 1st Duke of Ormonde"). He suggested that [Irish peers](/wiki/Peerage_of_Ireland "Peerage of Ireland") such as Ormonde should have no precedence over English [commoners](/wiki/Commoners "Commoners").[[2]](#cite_note-ODNB-3) The debate over the Irish Cattle Bill marks the first time that Ashley began to break with the court over an issue of policy.[[2]](#cite_note-ODNB-3)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/0f/John_Locke_by_John_Greenhill.jpg/250px-John_Locke_by_John_Greenhill.jpg)](/wiki/File:John_Locke_by_John_Greenhill.jpg)

[John Locke](/wiki/John_Locke "John Locke")

In October 1666, Ashley met [John Locke](/wiki/John_Locke "John Locke"), an Oxford don living a quiet life as a scholar and physician, who would in time become his personal secretary and receive government appointments due to his patronage.[[2]](#cite_note-ODNB-3) Ashley had gone to Oxford, where his son was at college, but Ashley was seeking treatment for a [liver](/wiki/Liver "Liver") ailment. Locke was a practicing physician and experimentalist, as well as having a strong interest in philosophy. Ashley was impressed with Locke, and persuaded him to become part of his retinue. Locke had been looking for a career, and in May 1667 moved into Ashley's home[[7]](#cite_note-8) at [Exeter House](/wiki/Cecil_House "Cecil House") in London, where he served as consulting physician during several novel surgical procedures and recoveries.[[8]](#cite_note-9) Beginning in 1667, Shaftesbury and Locke worked closely on the *[Grand Model for the Province of Carolina](/wiki/Grand_Model_for_the_Province_of_Carolina "Grand Model for the Province of Carolina")* and its centrepiece, the *[Fundamental Constitutions of Carolina](/wiki/Fundamental_Constitutions_of_Carolina "Fundamental Constitutions of Carolina")*.

When Southampton died in May 1667, Ashley, as under-treasurer, was expected to succeed Southampton as Lord High Treasurer.[[2]](#cite_note-ODNB-3) King Charles, however, decided to replace Southampton with a nine-man Commission of the Treasury, headed by [the Duke of Albemarle](/wiki/George_Monck,_1st_Duke_of_Albemarle "George Monck, 1st Duke of Albemarle") as First Lord of the Treasury.[[2]](#cite_note-ODNB-3) Ashley was named as one of the nine Treasury Commissioners at this time.[[2]](#cite_note-ODNB-3)

The failures of the English during the Second Anglo-Dutch War led Charles II to lose faith in the Earl of Clarendon, who was dismissed as Lord Chancellor on 31 August 1667.[[2]](#cite_note-ODNB-3) The court then moved to [impeach](/wiki/Impeach "Impeach") Clarendon, and was supported in this by many of Ashley's former political allies (including the [Duke of Buckingham](/wiki/George_Villiers,_2nd_Duke_of_Buckingham "George Villiers, 2nd Duke of Buckingham"), the [Earl of Bristol](/wiki/George_Digby,_2nd_Earl_of_Bristol "George Digby, 2nd Earl of Bristol"), and Sir Henry Bennett, who by this point had been created [Henry Bennet, 1st Earl of Arlington](/wiki/Henry_Bennet,_1st_Earl_of_Arlington "Henry Bennet, 1st Earl of Arlington")).[[2]](#cite_note-ODNB-3) Ashley, however, refused to join in the fight against Clarendon, opposing a motion to have Clarendon committed to the [Tower of London](/wiki/Tower_of_London "Tower of London") on a charge of [treason](/wiki/Treason "Treason").[[2]](#cite_note-ODNB-3) In 1667, Ashley was a signatory to *The Several Declarations of The Company of Royal Adventurers of England Trading into Africa*, a document published in 1667 which led to the creation of the [Royal Africa Company](/wiki/Royal_African_Company "Royal African Company").[[9]](#cite_note-10)

After the fall of Lord Clarendon in 1667, Lord Ashley became a prominent member of the [Cabal](/wiki/Cabal_Ministry "Cabal Ministry"), in which he formed the second "A".[[2]](#cite_note-ODNB-3) Although the term "Cabal Ministry" is used by historians, in reality, the five members of the Cabal ([Clifford](/wiki/Thomas_Clifford,_1st_Baron_Clifford_of_Chudleigh "Thomas Clifford, 1st Baron Clifford of Chudleigh"), [Arlington](/wiki/Henry_Bennet,_1st_Earl_of_Arlington "Henry Bennet, 1st Earl of Arlington"), [Buckingham](/wiki/George_Villiers,_2nd_Duke_of_Buckingham "George Villiers, 2nd Duke of Buckingham"), Ashley, and [Lauderdale](/wiki/John_Maitland,_1st_Duke_of_Lauderdale "John Maitland, 1st Duke of Lauderdale")) never formed a coherent ministerial team.[[2]](#cite_note-ODNB-3) In the period immediately after the fall of Clarendon, the government was dominated by Arlington and Buckingham. Ashley was out of royal favour and not admitted to the most powerful group of royal advisors, the Privy Council's committee on foreign affairs.[[2]](#cite_note-ODNB-3) Nevertheless, Ashley joined Arlington and Buckingham, as well as [John Wilkins](/wiki/John_Wilkins "John Wilkins"), [Bishop of Chester](/wiki/Bishop_of_Chester "Bishop of Chester"), in introducing government-backed bills in October 1667 and February 1668 to include moderate dissenters within the [Church of England](/wiki/Church_of_England "Church of England").[[2]](#cite_note-ODNB-3) Nothing came of these bills, however.[[2]](#cite_note-ODNB-3) In January 1668, the Privy Council's committees were reorganised, but Ashley retained a prominent position on the committee for trade and plantations.[[2]](#cite_note-ODNB-3)

In May 1668, Ashley became ill, apparently with a [hydatid cyst](/wiki/Hydatid_cyst "Hydatid cyst").[[2]](#cite_note-ODNB-3) His secretary, John Locke, recommended an operation that almost certainly saved Ashley's life and Ashley was grateful to Locke for the rest of his life.[[2]](#cite_note-ODNB-3) As part of the operation, a tube was inserted to drain fluid from the [abscess](/wiki/Abscess "Abscess"), and after the operation, the physician left the tube in the body, and installed a copper tap to allow for possible future drainage.[[2]](#cite_note-ODNB-3) In later years, this would be the occasion for his Tory enemies to dub him "Tapski", with the [Polish ending](/wiki/Polish_name "Polish name") because Tories accused him of wanting to make England an [elective monarchy](/wiki/Elective_monarchy "Elective monarchy") like the [Polish–Lithuanian Commonwealth](/wiki/Polish%E2%80%93Lithuanian_Commonwealth "Polish–Lithuanian Commonwealth").[[2]](#cite_note-ODNB-3)

In 1669, Ashley supported Arlington and Buckingham's proposal for a [political union](/wiki/Political_union "Political union") of England with the [Kingdom of Scotland](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Scotland "Kingdom of Scotland"). This proposal foundered when the Scottish insisted on equal representation with the English in parliament.[[2]](#cite_note-ODNB-3) Ashley probably did not support the [Conventicles Act 1670](/wiki/Conventicles_Act_1670 "Conventicles Act 1670"), but neither did he sign the formal protest against the passage of the act either.[[2]](#cite_note-ODNB-3)

Ashley, in his role as one of the eight [Lords Proprietor](/wiki/Lords_Proprietor "Lords Proprietor") of the [Province of Carolina](/wiki/Province_of_Carolina "Province of Carolina"), along with his secretary, [John Locke](/wiki/John_Locke "John Locke"), drafted the *[Fundamental Constitutions of Carolina](/wiki/Fundamental_Constitutions_of_Carolina "Fundamental Constitutions of Carolina")*, which were adopted by the eight Lords Proprietor in March 1669.[[2]](#cite_note-ODNB-3)

By this point, it had become obvious that the queen, Catherine of Braganza, was [barren](/wiki/Female_infertility "Female infertility") and would never produce an [heir](/wiki/Heir "Heir"), making the king's brother, [James, Duke of York](/wiki/James_II_of_England "James II of England") heir to the throne. This worried Ashley because he suspected that James was a Roman Catholic.[[2]](#cite_note-ODNB-3) Ashley and Buckingham urged Charles to declare his illegitimate son, the Duke of Monmouth, legitimate, as did [Charles Howard, 1st Earl of Carlisle](/wiki/Charles_Howard,_1st_Earl_of_Carlisle "Charles Howard, 1st Earl of Carlisle").[[2]](#cite_note-ODNB-3) When it became clear that the king would not do so, they urged him to divorce Catherine and remarry.[[2]](#cite_note-ODNB-3) This was the background to the famous Roos divorce case: [John Manners, Lord Roos](/wiki/John_Manners,_1st_Duke_of_Rutland "John Manners, 1st Duke of Rutland") had obtained a [separation from bed and board](/wiki/Legal_separation "Legal separation") from his wife in 1663, after he discovered she was committing [adultery](/wiki/Adultery "Adultery"). He had also been granted a divorce by an [ecclesiastical court](/wiki/Ecclesiastical_court "Ecclesiastical court") and had Lady Roos' children declared [illegitimate](/wiki/Illegitimate "Illegitimate"). In March 1670, Lord Roos asked Parliament to allow him to remarry. The debate on the Roos divorce bill became politically charged because it had an impact on whether Parliament could legally allow Charles to remarry.[[2]](#cite_note-ODNB-3) During the debate, Ashley spoke out strongly in favour of the Roos divorce bill, arguing that marriage was a civil contract, not a [sacrament](/wiki/Sacrament "Sacrament").[[2]](#cite_note-ODNB-3) Parliament ultimately gave Lord Roos permission to remarry, but Charles II never attempted to divorce his wif.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/69/Mignard%2C_possibly_after_-_Henrietta_of_England_-_National_Portrait_Gallery.jpg/250px-Mignard%2C_possibly_after_-_Henrietta_of_England_-_National_Portrait_Gallery.jpg)](/wiki/File:Mignard,_possibly_after_-_Henrietta_of_England_-_National_Portrait_Gallery.jpg)

[Princess Henrietta of England](/wiki/Princess_Henrietta_of_England "Princess Henrietta of England") (1644–1670), sister of Charles II, who arranged the [Secret Treaty of Dover](/wiki/Secret_Treaty_of_Dover "Secret Treaty of Dover") in May 1670; Ashley was not told about the Catholic clauses contained in the Secret Treaty of Dover, and, to fool Ashley, Buckingham, and Lauderdale, a second, public Treaty of Dover was signed in December 1670

Ashley did not know about the [Secret Treaty of Dover](/wiki/Secret_Treaty_of_Dover "Secret Treaty of Dover"), arranged by Charles II's sister [Henrietta Anne Stuart](/wiki/Henrietta_Anne_Stuart "Henrietta Anne Stuart") and signed on 22 May 1670, whereby Charles II concluded an alliance with [Louis XIV of France](/wiki/Louis_XIV_of_France "Louis XIV of France") against the [Dutch Republic](/wiki/Dutch_Republic "Dutch Republic"). Under the terms of the Secret Treaty of Dover, Charles would receive an annual subsidy from France (to enable him to govern without calling a parliament) in exchange for a promise that he would convert to Catholicism and re-Catholicize England at an unspecified future date.[[2]](#cite_note-ODNB-3) Of the members of the Cabal, only Arlington and Clifford were aware of the Catholic Clauses contained in the secret treaty.[[2]](#cite_note-ODNB-3) For the benefit of Ashley, Buckingham, and Lauderdale, Charles II arranged a mock treaty (*traité simulé*), concluding an alliance with France. Although he was suspicious of Catholic France, Ashley was also wary of Dutch commercial competition, and he, therefore, signed the mock Treaty of Dover on 21 December 1670.[[2]](#cite_note-ODNB-3)

Throughout 1671, Ashley argued in favour of reducing the [duty](/wiki/Duty_(economics) "Duty (economics)") on sugar imports, arguing that the duty would have an adverse effect on colonial sugar planters.[[2]](#cite_note-ODNB-3)

In September 1671, Ashley and Clifford oversaw a massive reform of England's customs system, whereby customs farmers were replaced with royal commissioners responsible for collecting customs.[[2]](#cite_note-ODNB-3) This change was ultimately to the benefit of the crown, but it caused a short-term loss of revenues that led to the [Great Stop of the Exchequer](/wiki/Great_Stop_of_the_Exchequer "Great Stop of the Exchequer").[[2]](#cite_note-ODNB-3) Ashley was widely blamed for the Great Stop of the Exchequer, although Clifford was the chief advocate of stopping the exchequer and Ashley in fact opposed the move.[[2]](#cite_note-ODNB-3)

In early 1672, with the [Third Anglo–Dutch War](/wiki/Third_Anglo%E2%80%93Dutch_War "Third Anglo–Dutch War") looming, many in the government feared that Protestant dissenters in England would form a [fifth column](/wiki/Fifth_column "Fifth column") and support their Dutch co-religionists against England.[[2]](#cite_note-ODNB-3) In an attempt to conciliate the Nonconformists, on 15 March 1672, Charles II issued his [Royal Declaration of Indulgence](/wiki/Declaration_of_Indulgence_(1672) "Declaration of Indulgence (1672)"), suspending the penal laws that punished non-attendance at Church of England services. Ashley strongly supported this Declaration.[[2]](#cite_note-ODNB-3)

According to the terms of the Treaty of Dover, England declared war on the Dutch Republic on 7 April 1672, thus launching the Third Anglo-Dutch War.[[2]](#cite_note-ODNB-3) To accompany the outbreak of the war, Charles issued a new round of honours, as part of which Ashley was named **Earl of Shaftesbury** and **Baron Cooper** on 23 April 1672.[[2]](#cite_note-ODNB-3)

In autumn 1672, Shaftesbury played a key role in setting up the [Bahamas](/wiki/Bahamas "Bahamas") Adventurers' Company.[[2]](#cite_note-ODNB-3)

#### Lord Chancellor, 1672–1673

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Anthony_Ashley_Cooper,_1st_Earl_of_Shaftesbury&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Lord Chancellor, 1672–1673")]

On 17 November 1672, the king named Shaftesbury [Lord Chancellor](/wiki/Lord_Chancellor "Lord Chancellor") of England,[[2]](#cite_note-ODNB-3) with [Sir John Duncombe](/wiki/John_Duncombe_(Bury_St_Edmunds_MP) "John Duncombe (Bury St Edmunds MP)") replacing Shaftesbury as [Chancellor of the Exchequer](/wiki/Chancellor_of_the_Exchequer "Chancellor of the Exchequer"). Shaftesbury was the last person without any training in the [common law](/wiki/Common_law "Common law") to be appointed to that position (until the appointment of [Liz Truss](/wiki/Liz_Truss "Liz Truss") in 2016).[[10]](#cite_note-Baker_Page_117-11) As Lord Chancellor, he addressed the opening of a new session of the [Cavalier Parliament](/wiki/Cavalier_Parliament "Cavalier Parliament") on 4 February 1673, calling on parliament to vote funds sufficient to carry out the war, arguing that the Dutch were the enemy of the monarchy and England's only major trade rival, and therefore had to be destroyed (at one point he exclaimed "[Delenda est Carthago](/wiki/Carthago_delenda_est "Carthago delenda est")"); defending the [Great Stop of the Exchequer](/wiki/Great_Stop_of_the_Exchequer "Great Stop of the Exchequer"); and arguing in support of the Royal Declaration of Indulgence.[[2]](#cite_note-ODNB-3)

Shaftesbury was not, however, well received by the House of Commons. One of Shaftesbury's old Dorset rivals, Colonel [Giles Strangways](/wiki/Giles_Strangways "Giles Strangways"), led an attack on writs of election that Lord Chancellor Shaftesbury had issued to fill 36 vacant seats in the House of Commons; Strangways argued that Shaftesbury was attempting to pack the Commons with his supporters and that only the [Speaker of the House](/wiki/List_of_Speakers_of_the_House_of_Commons_of_England "List of Speakers of the House of Commons of England") could issue writs to fill the vacant seats.[[2]](#cite_note-ODNB-3) The House of Commons agreed with Strangways and declared the elections void and the seats vacant.[[2]](#cite_note-ODNB-3) Furthermore, the Commons attacked the Declaration of Indulgence and demanded its withdrawal.[[2]](#cite_note-ODNB-3) Charles ultimately withdrew the address and cancelled the Declaration of Indulgence.[[2]](#cite_note-ODNB-3)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/78/Shaftesbury1672.jpg/250px-Shaftesbury1672.jpg)](/wiki/File:Shaftesbury1672.jpg)

Shaftesbury in the robes of the [Lord Chancellor](/wiki/Lord_Chancellor "Lord Chancellor"), ca. 1672–1673

The Commons then passed an address condemning the growth of [popery](/wiki/Popery "Popery") in England.[[2]](#cite_note-ODNB-3) To shore up the Protestantism of the nation, Parliament passed the [Test Act 1673](/wiki/Test_Act_1673 "Test Act 1673"), which became law on 20 March 1673.[[2]](#cite_note-ODNB-3) The Test Act required all holders of civil and military office in England to take [communion](/wiki/Eucharist "Eucharist") in the Church of England at least once a year and to make a declaration renouncing the Catholic doctrine of [transubstantiation](/wiki/Transubstantiation "Transubstantiation").[[2]](#cite_note-ODNB-3) Shaftesbury supported the Test Act, and, alongside [James Scott, 1st Duke of Monmouth](/wiki/James_Scott,_1st_Duke_of_Monmouth "James Scott, 1st Duke of Monmouth"), received the sacrament at [St Clement Danes](/wiki/St_Clement_Danes "St Clement Danes"), with John Locke serving as the legal witness for each man's conformity with the Test Act.[[2]](#cite_note-ODNB-3) In March 1673, Shaftesbury supported a bill for easing the plight of the Protestant dissenters in England, but nothing came of this bill.[[2]](#cite_note-ODNB-3)

Following the failure of the Declaration of Indulgence and the passage of the Test Act, it was obvious to all that the Cabal Ministry's days were numbered.[[2]](#cite_note-ODNB-3) Shaftesbury moved closer to the parliamentary opposition during this period, and became a supporter of ending the Third Anglo-Dutch War.[[2]](#cite_note-ODNB-3)

The Duke of York failed to take the Anglican sacrament at Easter 1673, further heightening Shaftesbury's concern that he was secretly a Catholic.[[2]](#cite_note-ODNB-3) Shaftesbury was initially mollified by the fact that both of the Duke of York's daughters, [Mary](/wiki/Mary_II_of_England "Mary II of England") and [Anne](/wiki/Anne,_Queen_of_Great_Britain "Anne, Queen of Great Britain"), were committed Protestants.[[2]](#cite_note-ODNB-3) However, in autumn 1673, the Duke of York married the Catholic [Mary of Modena](/wiki/Mary_of_Modena "Mary of Modena") by proxy, thus raising the possibility that James might have a son who would succeed to the throne ahead of Mary and Anne and thus give rise to a succession of Catholic monarchs.[[2]](#cite_note-ODNB-3) York urged the king to [prorogue](/wiki/Legislative_session#Procedure_in_Commonwealth_realms "Legislative session") parliament before it could vote on a motion condemning his marriage to Mary of Modena, but Shaftesbury used procedural techniques in the House of Lords to ensure that parliament continued sitting long enough to allow the House of Commons to pass a motion condemning the match.[[2]](#cite_note-ODNB-3) Shaftesbury, Arlington, [James Butler, 1st Duke of Ormonde](/wiki/James_Butler,_1st_Duke_of_Ormonde "James Butler, 1st Duke of Ormonde"), and [Henry Coventry](/wiki/Henry_Coventry "Henry Coventry") all urged Charles II to divorce Catherine of Braganza and remarry a Protestant princess.[[2]](#cite_note-ODNB-3) York began denouncing Shaftesbury to Charles II, and Charles II decided to remove Shaftesbury from his post as Lord Chancellor.[[2]](#cite_note-ODNB-3) On 9 November 1673, Henry Coventry travelled to [Exeter House](/wiki/Exeter_House "Exeter House") to inform Shaftesbury that he was relieved of his post as Lord Chancellor, but also issuing him a royal pardon for all crimes committed before 5 November 1673.[[2]](#cite_note-ODNB-3)

#### Opposition to Catholicism and break with Charles II, 1673–1674

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Anthony_Ashley_Cooper,_1st_Earl_of_Shaftesbury&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: Opposition to Catholicism and break with Charles II, 1673–1674")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/6c/Denzil_Holles_Old.jpg/250px-Denzil_Holles_Old.jpg)](/wiki/File:Denzil_Holles_Old.jpg)

[Denzil Holles, 1st Baron Holles](/wiki/Denzil_Holles,_1st_Baron_Holles "Denzil Holles, 1st Baron Holles") (1599–1680), whose London home was used by opposition peers to plan a strategy to counter the growth of Catholic influence in England

Following Shaftesbury's fall from royal favour, Arlington attempted to effect a reconciliation, in November 1673 convincing the French ambassador to offer Shaftesbury a bribe in exchange for supporting the French party at court.[[2]](#cite_note-ODNB-3) Shaftesbury refused this offer, saying he could never support "an interest that was so apparently destructive to [England's] religion and trade".[[2]](#cite_note-ODNB-3) Instead, he allied himself with the Spanish party at court, and urged peace with the Netherlands.[[2]](#cite_note-ODNB-3) He also continued to urge the king to divorce and remarry.[[2]](#cite_note-ODNB-3)

In the session of the [Cavalier Parliament](/wiki/Cavalier_Parliament "Cavalier Parliament") that began on 7 January 1674, Shaftesbury led the charge to keep England free from [popery](/wiki/Popery "Popery").[[2]](#cite_note-ODNB-3) He coordinated his efforts with a group of other peers who were displeased with the possibility of a Catholic succession; this group met at the home of [Denzil Holles, 1st Baron Holles](/wiki/Denzil_Holles,_1st_Baron_Holles "Denzil Holles, 1st Baron Holles"), and included [Charles Howard, 1st Earl of Carlisle](/wiki/Charles_Howard,_1st_Earl_of_Carlisle "Charles Howard, 1st Earl of Carlisle"), [Thomas Belasyse, 2nd Viscount Fauconberg](/wiki/Thomas_Belasyse,_1st_Earl_Fauconberg "Thomas Belasyse, 1st Earl Fauconberg"), [James Cecil, 3rd Earl of Salisbury](/wiki/James_Cecil,_3rd_Earl_of_Salisbury "James Cecil, 3rd Earl of Salisbury"), [George Villiers, 2nd Duke of Buckingham](/wiki/George_Villiers,_2nd_Duke_of_Buckingham "George Villiers, 2nd Duke of Buckingham"), and [George Savile, 1st Viscount Halifax](/wiki/George_Savile,_1st_Marquess_of_Halifax "George Savile, 1st Marquess of Halifax").[[2]](#cite_note-ODNB-3) On 8 January 1674, Shaftesbury gave a speech in the [House of Lords](/wiki/House_of_Lords "House of Lords") warning that the 16,000 Catholics living in London were on the verge of rebellion, which caused the Lords to pass an address expelling all Catholics from within 10 miles of London.[[2]](#cite_note-ODNB-3) On 12 January, he introduced a measure that would require every peer, including the Duke of York, to take the [Oath of Allegiance](/wiki/Oath_of_Allegiance_(United_Kingdom) "Oath of Allegiance (United Kingdom)") renouncing the pope and recognising the [royal supremacy](/wiki/Royal_supremacy "Royal supremacy") in the church (the oath was first required by the [Popish Recusants Act 1605](/wiki/Popish_Recusants_Act_1605 "Popish Recusants Act 1605")).[[2]](#cite_note-ODNB-3) On 24 January, the Earl of Salisbury introduced a bill requiring that any children of the Duke of York should be raised as Protestants.[[2]](#cite_note-ODNB-3) His proposed legislation further provided that neither the king nor any prince of the blood could marry a Catholic without parliamentary consent, on pain of being excluded from the royal succession.[[2]](#cite_note-ODNB-3) Shaftesbury spoke forcefully in favour of Salisbury's proposal; he was opposed by the bishops and [Lord Finch](/wiki/Heneage_Finch,_1st_Earl_of_Nottingham "Heneage Finch, 1st Earl of Nottingham").[[2]](#cite_note-ODNB-3) By February, the opposition lords were considering accusing the Duke of York of [high treason](/wiki/High_treason_in_the_United_Kingdom "High treason in the United Kingdom"), which resulted in the king [proroguing](/wiki/Legislative_session#Procedure_in_Commonwealth_realms "Legislative session") parliament on 24 February to protect his brother.[[2]](#cite_note-ODNB-3)

Shaftesbury's actions in the 1674 session further angered Charles II; on 19 May 1674, Shaftesbury was expelled from the Privy Council, and subsequently sacked as [Lord Lieutenant of Dorset](/wiki/Lord_Lieutenant_of_Dorset "Lord Lieutenant of Dorset") and ordered to leave London.[[2]](#cite_note-ODNB-3)

#### Leader of Opposition to Danby, 1674–1678

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Anthony_Ashley_Cooper,_1st_Earl_of_Shaftesbury&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: Leader of Opposition to Danby, 1674–1678")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/fb/1stDukeOfLeeds.jpg/250px-1stDukeOfLeeds.jpg)](/wiki/File:1stDukeOfLeeds.jpg)

[Thomas Osborne, Earl of Danby](/wiki/Thomas_Osborne,_1st_Duke_of_Leeds "Thomas Osborne, 1st Duke of Leeds") (1631–1712), who became Charles II's main adviser following the fall of the [Cabal Ministry](/wiki/Cabal_Ministry "Cabal Ministry"), and who drew support from former [Cavaliers](/wiki/Cavaliers "Cavaliers") and the supporters of the established [Church of England](/wiki/Church_of_England "Church of England")

Charles II now turned to [Thomas Osborne, Earl of Danby](/wiki/Thomas_Osborne,_1st_Duke_of_Leeds "Thomas Osborne, 1st Duke of Leeds"). Danby proceeded to freeze out peers who had collaborated during the Cromwellian regime and promoted former royalists.[[2]](#cite_note-ODNB-3) Danby was a champion of the Church of England who favoured strict interpretation of the penal laws against both Catholics and Protestant Nonconformists.[[2]](#cite_note-ODNB-3)

On 3 February 1675, Shaftesbury wrote a letter to Carlisle in which he argued that the king needed to dissolve the [Cavalier Parliament](/wiki/Cavalier_Parliament "Cavalier Parliament"), which had been elected in early 1661, and call fresh elections.[[2]](#cite_note-ODNB-3) He argued that frequent parliamentary elections were in the best interest of both the crown and the people of England.[[2]](#cite_note-ODNB-3) This letter circulated widely in manuscript form.[[2]](#cite_note-ODNB-3)

The Duke of York was opposed to Danby's strict enforcement of the penal laws against Catholics, and by April 1675, he had reached out to Shaftesbury to make a truce between them whereby they would be united in opposition to Danby's brand of Anglican royalism.[[2]](#cite_note-ODNB-3) In late April 1675, Danby introduced a Test Oath by which all holding office or seats in either House of Parliament were to declare resistance to the royal power a crime, and promise to abstain from all attempts to alter the government of either church or state.[[2]](#cite_note-ODNB-3) Shaftesbury led the parliamentary opposition to Danby's Test Bill, arguing that, under certain circumstances, it was lawful to resist the king's ministers, and that, as in the case of the [Protestant Reformation](/wiki/Protestant_Reformation "Protestant Reformation"), it was sometimes necessary to alter the church so as to restore it.[[2]](#cite_note-ODNB-3)

In spite of Shaftesbury's eloquence, his view remained the minority view in the parliament, forcing the king to prorogue parliament on 9 June 1675 to avoid the passage of the bill.[[2]](#cite_note-ODNB-3) The Duke of York, grateful for Shaftesbury's assistance in the debate against Danby's bill, now attempted to reconcile Shaftesbury with the king, and Shaftesbury was admitted to kiss the king's hand on 13 June 1675.[[2]](#cite_note-ODNB-3) This, however, angered Danby, who intervened with the king, and on 24 June, the king again ordered Shaftesbury to leave court.[[2]](#cite_note-ODNB-3)

In 1675, following the death of Sir Giles Strangways, MP for Dorset, Shaftesbury initially endorsed Lord Digby, son of [George Digby, 2nd Earl of Bristol](/wiki/George_Digby,_2nd_Earl_of_Bristol "George Digby, 2nd Earl of Bristol"), for the seat but, upon learning that Digby was a strong supporter of the court, he decided to back [Thomas Moore](/wiki/Thomas_Moore_(died_1695) "Thomas Moore (died 1695)"), who was the chief supporter of [conventicles](/wiki/Conventicles "Conventicles") in the county.[[2]](#cite_note-ODNB-3) This led to Shaftesbury making an enemy of both Digby and Bristol, who accused him of supporting sedition and faction and wanting a return of the [English Commonwealth](/wiki/English_Commonwealth "English Commonwealth").[[2]](#cite_note-ODNB-3)
