[John Locke](/wiki/John_Locke "John Locke") (1632–1704), who probably participated in writing *A Letter from a Person of Quality to his Friend in the Country* (1675)

[![logo](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/4c/Wikisource-logo.svg/40px-Wikisource-logo.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Wikisource-logo.svg)

English [Wikisource](/wiki/Wikisource "Wikisource") has original text related to this article:

**[The Earl of Shaftsbury's Speech in the House of Lords, upon the Debate of Appointing a Day for the Hearing Dr. Shirley's Cause, 20 October 1675](https://en.wikisource.org/wiki/en:The_Earl_of_Shaftsbury%27s_Speech_in_the_House_of_Lords,_upon_the_Debate_of_Appointing_a_Day_for_the_Hearing_Dr._Shirley%27s_Cause,_20_October_1675 "s:en:The Earl of Shaftsbury's Speech in the House of Lords, upon the Debate of Appointing a Day for the Hearing Dr. Shirley's Cause, 20 October 1675")**

In the summer of 1675, Shaftesbury wrote a 15,000-word pamphlet entitled *A Letter from a Person of Quality to his Friend in the Country* denouncing Danby's Test Bill.[[2]](#cite_note-ODNB-3) Shaftesbury's secretary and advisor, [John Locke](/wiki/John_Locke "John Locke"), played a role in drafting the *Letter*, and for a time it was attributed to Locke. It is unclear what Locke's role was, whether solely as [amanuensis](/wiki/Amanuensis "Amanuensis") or in a more active role of collaboration, perhaps even as [ghostwriter](/wiki/Ghostwriter "Ghostwriter"). Reaction to it was swift, with the House of Lords ordering it to be burned and set up a committee to uncover who printed it.[[2]](#cite_note-ODNB-3)[[11]](#cite_note-12) The *Letter* argued that since the time of [the Restoration](/wiki/English_Restoration "English Restoration"), "the High Episcopal Man, and the Old [Cavalier](/wiki/Cavalier "Cavalier")" (now led by Danby) had conspired to make "the Government [absolute](/wiki/Absolute_monarchy "Absolute monarchy") and arbitrary."[[2]](#cite_note-ODNB-3) According to the *Letter*, this (Church) party was attempting to establish [divine right monarchy](/wiki/Divine_right_of_kings "Divine right of kings") and divine right episcopacy, meaning that neither the king nor the bishops could be constrained by the [rule of law](/wiki/Rule_of_law "Rule of law").[[2]](#cite_note-ODNB-3) Religion was the overwhelming concern of the Letter, especially the attack upon Protestant freedoms occurring in England. By allying with the monarchy the old Cavaliers would achieve 'the aims of the high church through the Act of Uniformity (1662)', threatening dissenters with the Declaration of Indulgence.[[12]](#cite_note-mans-13)

Danby's Test Oath proposal was merely the latest, most nefarious attempt to introduce divine right monarchy and episcopacy on the country. The Letter went on to describe the debates of the House of Lords during the last session, setting forth the arguments that Shaftesbury and other lords used in opposition to Danby and the bishops. This letter was published anonymously in November 1675, and quickly became a best-seller, in no small part because it was one of the first books ever to inform the public about the debates that occurred within the House of Lords.[[2]](#cite_note-ODNB-3)

Shaftesbury repeated the accusations of the *Letter from a Person of Quality* on the floor of the House of Lords during the parliamentary session of October–November 1675.[[2]](#cite_note-ODNB-3) During the debate on the case of *Shirley v. Fagg*, a jurisdictional dispute about whether the House of Lords could hear appeals from lower courts when the case involved members of the House of Commons, Shaftesbury gave a celebrated speech on 20 October 1675.[[2]](#cite_note-ODNB-3) He argued that Danby and the bishops were attempting to neuter the power of the House of Lords.[[2]](#cite_note-ODNB-3)

Shaftesbury argued that every king could only rule either through the [nobility](/wiki/Nobility "Nobility") or through a [standing army](/wiki/Standing_army "Standing army"); thus, this attempt to restrict the power of the nobility was part of a plot to rule the country through a standing army.[[2]](#cite_note-ODNB-3) He argued that the bishops believed that the king was king by [divine right](/wiki/Divine_right_of_kings "Divine right of kings"), not by [law](/wiki/Constitutional_monarchy "Constitutional monarchy") and that, if the bishops' propositions were taken to their logical conclusion, "our [Magna Charta](/wiki/Magna_Charta "Magna Charta") is of no force, our Laws are but Rules amongst our selves during the Kings pleasure" and "All the Properties and Liberties of the People, are to give away, not onley to the interest, but the will and pleasure of the Crown".[[2]](#cite_note-ODNB-3) Shaftesbury's concerns were rooted in his Civil War and Commonwealth experiences, in which he believed that Cromwell's reliance upon the army to assert his authority had been tyrannical. Moreover, the use of the army during that period had engendered a "mechanic tyranny" that enabled popular elements (within the army) to pull England towards democratic power: something to be feared and avoided.[[12]](#cite_note-mans-13)

On 20 November 1675, Shaftesbury seconded a motion by [Charles Mohun, 3rd Baron Mohun of Okehampton](/w/index.php?title=Charles_Mohun,_3rd_Baron_Mohun_of_Okehampton&action=edit&redlink=1 "Charles Mohun, 3rd Baron Mohun of Okehampton (page does not exist)") calling on the king to end the dispute of *Shirley v. Fagg* by [dissolving parliament](/wiki/Dissolution_of_parliament "Dissolution of parliament").[[2]](#cite_note-ODNB-3) This motion, which was supported by the Duke of York and the Catholic peers, was defeated by a vote of 50–48, prompting Shaftesbury and 21 other peers to enter a protest on the grounds that "according to the ancient Lawes and Statutes of this Realm ... there should be frequent and new Parliaments" and that the House of Commons was being unnecessarily obstructionist.[[2]](#cite_note-ODNB-3) Parliament was prorogued on 22 November 1675, with the prorogation saying that parliament would not sit again until 15 February 1677.[[2]](#cite_note-ODNB-3) Shortly thereafter, there appeared a pamphlet entitled *Two Seasonable Discourses Concerning the Present Parliament*, that argued that the king should call a new parliament because a new parliament would vote the king money, preserve the Church of England, introduce [religious toleration](/wiki/Religious_toleration "Religious toleration") for the Nonconformists, and deliver Catholics from the penal laws in an exchange for Catholics being deprived of access to court, holding office, and the right to bear arms.[[2]](#cite_note-ODNB-3)

In mid-February 1676, Charles sent his [Secretary of State for the Southern Department](/wiki/Secretary_of_State_for_the_Southern_Department "Secretary of State for the Southern Department"), [Sir Joseph Williamson](/wiki/Joseph_Williamson_(English_politician) "Joseph Williamson (English politician)") to tell Shaftesbury to leave town.[[2]](#cite_note-ODNB-3) Shaftesbury refused and continued to receive visits at [Exeter House](/wiki/Exeter_House "Exeter House") from opposition MPs and other discontented elements.[[2]](#cite_note-ODNB-3) Danby argued that Charles should order Shaftesbury arrested and sent to the [Tower of London](/wiki/Tower_of_London "Tower of London"), but Sir Joseph Williamson refused to sign the [warrant](/wiki/Warrant_(law) "Warrant (law)").[[2]](#cite_note-ODNB-3) In this period, Shaftesbury relocated from Exeter House to the less expensive [Thanet](/wiki/Isle_of_Thanet "Isle of Thanet") House.[[2]](#cite_note-ODNB-3)

On 24 June 1676, during the election of the [Sheriffs of the City of London](/wiki/Sheriffs_of_the_City_of_London "Sheriffs of the City of London") at the [Guildhall](/wiki/Guildhall,_London "Guildhall, London"), [linen](/wiki/Linen "Linen") [draper](/wiki/Draper "Draper") Francis Jenks gave a sensational speech arguing that two statutes from the reign of [Edward III](/wiki/Edward_III_of_England "Edward III of England") required that parliament sit every year, and that by proroguing the Cavalier Parliament until 15 February 1677 (meaning no session would be held in 1676 at all), the king had inadvertently dissolved parliament and that the Cavalier Parliament was now legally dissolved.[[2]](#cite_note-ODNB-3) Although Buckingham, not Shaftesbury, was behind Jenks' speech, many suspected Shaftesbury's involvement; after Jenks' speech, Shaftesbury decided to take full advantage of the argument, arranging with his allies for a number of pamphlets arguing the case.[[2]](#cite_note-ODNB-3) One of these pamphlets, *Some considerations upon the question, whether the parliament is dissolved, by its prorogation for 15 months?* argued that parliament had the authority to restrict the royal prerogative and could even "bind, limit, restrain and govern the Descent and Inheritance of the Crown it self."[[2]](#cite_note-ODNB-3) The Duke of York was furious at the inclusion of this argument; Buckingham told York that Shaftesbury had drafted the controversial passage, but Shaftesbury claimed that the passage was inserted in the pamphlet without his knowledge.[[2]](#cite_note-ODNB-3)

When parliament finally met on 15 February 1677, Buckingham, backed by Shaftesbury, Salisbury, and [Philip Wharton, 4th Baron Wharton](/wiki/Philip_Wharton,_4th_Baron_Wharton "Philip Wharton, 4th Baron Wharton"), introduced a motion declaring that, because of the 15-month prorogation, on the basis of the statutes from the reign of Edward III, no parliament was legally in existence.[[2]](#cite_note-ODNB-3) Parliament not only rejected this argument, but also resolved that the four peers had committed [Contempt of Parliament](/wiki/Contempt_of_Parliament "Contempt of Parliament") and should apologise.[[2]](#cite_note-ODNB-3) When the four refused, they were committed to the Tower of London.[[2]](#cite_note-ODNB-3) Shaftesbury petitioned for his release, and in June 1677, brought a [writ](/wiki/Writ "Writ") of [habeas corpus](/wiki/Habeas_corpus "Habeas corpus") before the [Court of King's Bench](/wiki/Court_of_King%27s_Bench_(England) "Court of King's Bench (England)").[[2]](#cite_note-ODNB-3) The court, however, determined that it lacked jurisdiction because Parliament, a superior court, was currently in session.[[2]](#cite_note-ODNB-3) Charles ordered Buckingham, Salisbury, and Wharton released from the Tower shortly thereafter, but Shaftesbury continued to refuse to apologise.[[2]](#cite_note-ODNB-3) Shaftesbury had grown increasingly suspicious of Charles II.[[2]](#cite_note-ODNB-3) Charles had begun raising an army, ostensibly for war with France, but Shaftesbury worried that Charles was really preparing to abolish parliament and rule the country with a [standing army](/wiki/Standing_army "Standing army") on the model of [Louis XIV of France](/wiki/Louis_XIV_of_France "Louis XIV of France").[[2]](#cite_note-ODNB-3) It was not until 25 February 1678 that Shaftesbury finally apologised to the king and to parliament for his support of the motion in the House of Lords and for bringing a writ of *habeas corpus* against Parliament.[[2]](#cite_note-ODNB-3)

With war with France looming, in March 1678, Shaftesbury, Buckingham, Holles, and Halifax spoke out in favour of immediately declaring war on France.[[2]](#cite_note-ODNB-3) Charles delayed declaring war, however, leading Shaftesbury to support a resolution of the House of Commons providing for immediately disbanding the army that Charles was raising.[[2]](#cite_note-ODNB-3) Charles prorogued parliament on 25 June, but the army was not disbanded, which worried Shaftesbury.[[2]](#cite_note-ODNB-3)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/dd/Titus_Oates.jpg/250px-Titus_Oates.jpg)](/wiki/File:Titus_Oates.jpg)

[Titus Oates](/wiki/Titus_Oates "Titus Oates") (1649–1705), whose accusations in autumn 1678 that there was a [Popish Plot](/wiki/Popish_Plot "Popish Plot") to murder the king and massacre English Protestants, set off a wave of anti-Catholic hysteria. Shaftesbury would play a prominent part in prosecuting the individuals whom Oates (falsely) accused of manufacturing this plot. The wave of anti-Catholic sentiment set off by Oates would be at the centre of Shaftesbury's political program during the [Exclusion Crisis](/wiki/Exclusion_Crisis "Exclusion Crisis").

In August and September 1678, [Titus Oates](/wiki/Titus_Oates "Titus Oates") made accusations that there was a [Popish Plot](/wiki/Popish_Plot "Popish Plot") to assassinate the king, overthrow the government, and massacre English Protestants.[[2]](#cite_note-ODNB-3) It was later revealed that Oates had simply made up most of the details of the plot, and that there was no elaborate Popish Plot. However, when Parliament re-convened on 21 October 1678, Oates had not yet been discredited and the Popish Plot was the major topic of concern. Shaftesbury was a member of all the important committees of the House of Lords designed to combat the Popish Plot.[[2]](#cite_note-ODNB-3) On 2 November 1678, he introduced a motion demanding that the Duke of York be removed from the king's presence, although this motion was never voted on.[[2]](#cite_note-ODNB-3) He supported the [Test Act 1678](/wiki/Test_Act_1678 "Test Act 1678"), which required that all peers and members of the House of Commons should make a declaration against [transubstantiation](/wiki/Transubstantiation "Transubstantiation"), [invocation of saints](/wiki/Intercession_of_saints "Intercession of saints"), and the sacrifice of the [mass](/wiki/Mass_(liturgy) "Mass (liturgy)"), effectively excluding all Catholics from Parliament.[[2]](#cite_note-ODNB-3) Oates had accused the queen, [Catherine of Braganza](/wiki/Catherine_of_Braganza "Catherine of Braganza"), of involvement in the Popish Plot, leading the House of Commons to pass a resolution calling for the queen and her retinue to be removed from court; when the House of Lords rejected this resolution, Shaftesbury entered a formal protest.[[2]](#cite_note-ODNB-3) Shaftesbury was now gaining a great reputation amongst the common people as a Protestant hero.[[2]](#cite_note-ODNB-3) On 9 November 1678, Charles promised that he would sign any bill that would make them safe during the reign of his successor, so long as they did not impeach the right of his successor; this speech was widely misreported as Charles's having agreed to name the Duke of Monmouth as his successor, leading to celebratory bonfires throughout London, with crowds drinking the health of "the King, the Duke of Monmouth, and Earl of Shaftesbury, as the only three pillars of all safety".[[2]](#cite_note-ODNB-3) The citizens of London, fearing a Catholic plot on Shaftesbury's life, paid for a special guard to protect him.[[2]](#cite_note-ODNB-3)

In December 1678, discussion turned to impeaching the Earl of Danby, and, to protect his minister, Charles II prorogued parliament on 30 December 1678.[[2]](#cite_note-ODNB-3) On 24 January 1679, Charles II finally dissolved the Cavalier Parliament, which had sat for 18 years.[[2]](#cite_note-ODNB-3)

#### The Exclusion Crisis and the birth of the Whig Party, 1679–1683

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Anthony_Ashley_Cooper,_1st_Earl_of_Shaftesbury&action=edit&section=13 "Edit section: The Exclusion Crisis and the birth of the Whig Party, 1679–1683")]

##### The Habeas Corpus Parliament, 1679

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Anthony_Ashley_Cooper,_1st_Earl_of_Shaftesbury&action=edit&section=14 "Edit section: The Habeas Corpus Parliament, 1679")]

In February 1679, elections were held for a new parliament, known to history as the [Habeas Corpus Parliament](/wiki/Habeas_Corpus_Parliament "Habeas Corpus Parliament").[[2]](#cite_note-ODNB-3) In preparation for this parliament, Shaftesbury drew up a list of members of the House of Commons in which he estimated that 32% of the members were friends of the court, 61% favoured the opposition, and 7% could go either way.[[2]](#cite_note-ODNB-3) He also drafted a pamphlet that was never published, entitled "The Present State of the Kingdom": in this pamphlet, Shaftesbury expressed concern about the power of France, the Popish Plot, and the bad influence exerted on the king by Danby, the [royal mistress](/wiki/Royal_mistress "Royal mistress") [Louise de Kérouaille, Duchess of Portsmouth](/wiki/Louise_de_K%C3%A9rouaille,_Duchess_of_Portsmouth "Louise de Kérouaille, Duchess of Portsmouth") (a Catholic), and the Duke of York, who according to Shaftesbury was now attempting "to introduce a military and arbitrary government in his brother's time".[[2]](#cite_note-ODNB-3)

[![logo](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/4c/Wikisource-logo.svg/40px-Wikisource-logo.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Wikisource-logo.svg)

English [Wikisource](/wiki/Wikisource "Wikisource") has original text related to this article:

**[The Earl of Shaftesbury's Speech in the House of Lords, 25 March 1679](https://en.wikisource.org/wiki/en:The_Earl_of_Shaftesbury%27s_Speech_in_the_House_of_Lords,_25_March_1679 "s:en:The Earl of Shaftesbury's Speech in the House of Lords, 25 March 1679")**

The new parliament met on 6 March 1679, and on 25 March, Shaftesbury delivered a dramatic address in the House of Lords in which he warned of the threat of popery and arbitrary government; denounced the royal administration in [Scotland](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Scotland "Kingdom of Scotland") under [John Maitland, 1st Duke of Lauderdale](/wiki/John_Maitland,_1st_Duke_of_Lauderdale "John Maitland, 1st Duke of Lauderdale"), and that in [Ireland](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Ireland "Kingdom of Ireland") under [James Butler, 1st Duke of Ormonde](/wiki/James_Butler,_1st_Duke_of_Ormonde "James Butler, 1st Duke of Ormonde"); and loudly denounced the policies of [Thomas Osborne, Earl of Danby](/wiki/Thomas_Osborne,_1st_Duke_of_Leeds "Thomas Osborne, 1st Duke of Leeds") in England.[[2]](#cite_note-ODNB-3) Shaftesbury supported the House of Commons when it introduced a [Bill of Attainder](/wiki/Bill_of_Attainder "Bill of Attainder") against Danby, and voted in favour of the bill in the House of Lords on 14 April 1679.[[2]](#cite_note-ODNB-3) Shaftesbury attempted to neutralise the influence of the episcopal bench in favour of Danby by introducing a bill moving that the bishops should not be able to sit in the House of Lords during capital trials.[[2]](#cite_note-ODNB-3)

##### Lord President of the Council, 1679

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Anthony_Ashley_Cooper,_1st_Earl_of_Shaftesbury&action=edit&section=15 "Edit section: Lord President of the Council, 1679")]

Charles II thought that Shaftesbury was mainly angry because he had been out of royal favour for long, and hoped that he could rein Shaftesbury in by naming him [Lord President of the Council](/wiki/Lord_President_of_the_Council "Lord President of the Council") on 21 April 1679, with a salary of £4,000 a year.[[2]](#cite_note-ODNB-3) Soon, however, Shaftesbury made it clear that he could not be bought off. During meetings of the now-reconstituted Privy Council, Shaftesbury repeatedly argued that the Duke of York must be excluded from the line of succession.[[2]](#cite_note-ODNB-3) He also continued to argue that Charles should remarry a Protestant princess, or legitimise [James Scott, 1st Duke of Monmouth](/wiki/James_Scott,_1st_Duke_of_Monmouth "James Scott, 1st Duke of Monmouth").[[2]](#cite_note-ODNB-3) During these meetings, [Arthur Capell, 1st Earl of Essex](/wiki/Arthur_Capell,_1st_Earl_of_Essex "Arthur Capell, 1st Earl of Essex") and [George Savile, 1st Earl of Halifax](/wiki/George_Savile,_1st_Marquess_of_Halifax "George Savile, 1st Marquess of Halifax") argued that the powers of a Catholic successor could be limited, but Shaftesbury argued that that would change "the whole government, and set up a democracy instead of a monarchy".[[2]](#cite_note-ODNB-3)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/6e/William_Russell_Lord_Russell.jpg/250px-William_Russell_Lord_Russell.jpg)](/wiki/File:William_Russell_Lord_Russell.jpg)

[William Russell, Lord Russell](/wiki/William_Russell,_Lord_Russell "William Russell, Lord Russell") (1639–1683) was one of Shaftesbury's closest political allies during the [Exclusion Crisis](/wiki/Exclusion_Crisis "Exclusion Crisis"); a leader in the House of Commons, he introduced the [Exclusion Bill](/wiki/Exclusion_Bill "Exclusion Bill") on 11 May 1679

On 11 May 1679, Shaftesbury's close political ally, [William Russell, Lord Russell](/wiki/William_Russell,_Lord_Russell "William Russell, Lord Russell"), introduced an Exclusion Bill in the House of Commons, which would have excluded the Duke of York from the succession.[[2]](#cite_note-ODNB-3) This bill passed first and second [reading](/wiki/Reading_(legislature) "Reading (legislature)") on 21 May 1679.[[2]](#cite_note-ODNB-3) To stop the Exclusion Bill and the Bill of Attainder directed at Danby, Charles II prorogued the parliament on 27 May 1679 and dissolved it on 3 July 1679, both of which moves infuriated Shaftesbury.[[2]](#cite_note-ODNB-3) As its name implies, the only achievement of the [Habeas Corpus Parliament](/wiki/Habeas_Corpus_Parliament "Habeas Corpus Parliament") was the passage of the [Habeas Corpus Act of 1679](/wiki/Habeas_Corpus_Act_1679 "Habeas Corpus Act 1679").[[2]](#cite_note-ODNB-3)

For the time being Shaftesbury retained his position on the Privy Council, and he and the Duke of Monmouth formed an alliance on the Council that was designed to be obstructionist.[[2]](#cite_note-ODNB-3) There were some disagreements between Shaftesbury and Monmouth: for example, Shaftesbury was critical of Monmouth's decision to crush a rebellion by Scottish [Covenanters](/wiki/Covenanters "Covenanters") quickly at the [Battle of Bothwell Brig](/wiki/Battle_of_Bothwell_Brig "Battle of Bothwell Brig") in June 1679, arguing that the rebellion should have been drawn out to force Charles II to recall parliament.[[2]](#cite_note-ODNB-3)

On 21 August 1679, the king fell ill, leading Essex and Halifax (who feared Monmouth was about to launch a coup) to ask the Duke of York, whom Charles had sent to [Brussels](/wiki/Brussels "Brussels") in late 1678, to return to England.[[2]](#cite_note-ODNB-3) Charles soon recovered and then ordered both York and Monmouth into exile.[[2]](#cite_note-ODNB-3) When Charles agreed to allow his brother to move from Flanders to Scotland in October 1679, Shaftesbury summoned an extraordinary meeting of the Privy Council to discuss the Duke's move, acting on his own authority as Lord President of the Council because the king was at [Newmarket](/wiki/Newmarket_Palace "Newmarket Palace") at the time.[[2]](#cite_note-ODNB-3) Angered by this insubordination, Charles removed Shaftesbury from the Privy Council on 14 October 1679.[[2]](#cite_note-ODNB-3)

##### The Exclusion Bill Parliament, 1679–1680

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Anthony_Ashley_Cooper,_1st_Earl_of_Shaftesbury&action=edit&section=16 "Edit section: The Exclusion Bill Parliament, 1679–1680")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/05/The_Solemn_Mock_Procession_of_the_Pope_%281829%29.jpg/250px-The_Solemn_Mock_Procession_of_the_Pope_%281829%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:The_Solemn_Mock_Procession_of_the_Pope_(1829).jpg)

"The Solemn Mock [Procession](/wiki/Procession "Procession") of the Pope, [Cardinals](/wiki/Cardinal_(Catholicism) "Cardinal (Catholicism)"), [Jesuits](/wiki/Jesuits "Jesuits"), [Friars](/wiki/Friars "Friars"), Etc. Through the City of London, 17 November 1679." Throughout the [Exclusion Crisis](/wiki/Exclusion_Crisis "Exclusion Crisis"), Shaftesbury's [Whig](/wiki/Whig_(British_political_faction) "Whig (British political faction)") allies in the [Green Ribbon Club](/wiki/Green_Ribbon_Club "Green Ribbon Club") engaged in anti-Catholic propaganda, such as mock processions, the climax of which involved burning the pope in [effigy](/wiki/Effigy "Effigy").

Elections for a new parliament, which ultimately came to be known as the Exclusion Bill Parliament, were held in summer 1679, but they went badly for the court, so, with parliament scheduled to meet in October 1679, Charles prorogued the parliament until 26 January 1680.[[2]](#cite_note-ODNB-3) Shaftesbury worried that the king might be intending to not meet this new parliament, so he launched a massive petitioning campaign to pressure the king to meet parliament.[[2]](#cite_note-ODNB-3) He wrote to the Duke of Monmouth, telling him that he should return from exile, and on 27 November 1679 Monmouth rode back into London amidst scenes of widespread celebration.[[2]](#cite_note-ODNB-3) On 7 December 1679, a petition signed by Shaftesbury and fifteen other [Whig](/wiki/Whig_(British_political_faction) "Whig (British political faction)") peers calling on Charles to meet parliament, followed up with a 20,000-name petition on 13 January 1680.[[2]](#cite_note-ODNB-3) However, instead of meeting parliament, Charles further prorogued parliament and recalled his brother from Scotland. Shaftesbury now urged his friends on the Privy Council to resign, and four did so.[[2]](#cite_note-ODNB-3)

On 24 March 1680, Shaftesbury told the Privy Council of information he had received that the Irish Catholics were about to launch a rebellion, backed by the French.[[2]](#cite_note-ODNB-3) Several privy councillors, especially [Henry Coventry](/wiki/Henry_Coventry "Henry Coventry"), thought that Shaftesbury was making the entire story up to inflame public opinion, but an investigation was launched.[[2]](#cite_note-ODNB-3) This investigation ultimately resulted in the execution of [Oliver Plunkett](/wiki/Oliver_Plunkett "Oliver Plunkett"), Catholic [Archbishop of Armagh](/wiki/Archbishop_of_Armagh "Archbishop of Armagh"), on trumped-up charges.[[2]](#cite_note-ODNB-3)

On 26 June 1680, Shaftesbury led a group of fifteen peers and commoners who presented an indictment to the [Middlesex](/wiki/Middlesex "Middlesex") [grand jury](/wiki/Grand_jury "Grand jury") in [Westminster Hall](/wiki/Westminster_Hall "Westminster Hall"), charging the Duke of York with being a popish [recusant](/wiki/Recusant "Recusant") in violation of the [penal laws](/wiki/Penal_law_(British) "Penal law (British)").[[2]](#cite_note-ODNB-3) Before the grand jury could act, they were dismissed for interfering in matters of state.[[2]](#cite_note-ODNB-3) The next week, Shaftesbury again tried to indict the Duke of York, but again the grand jury was dismissed before it could take any action.[[2]](#cite_note-ODNB-3)

The parliament finally met on 21 October 1680, and on 23 October, Shaftesbury called for a committee to be set up to investigate the Popish Plot.[[2]](#cite_note-ODNB-3) When the [Exclusion Bill](/wiki/Exclusion_Bill "Exclusion Bill") again came before the House of Lords, Shaftesbury gave an impassioned pro-Exclusion speech on 15 November.[[2]](#cite_note-ODNB-3) The Lords, however, rejected the Exclusion Bill by a vote of 63–30.[[2]](#cite_note-ODNB-3) The Lords now explored alternative ways of limiting the powers of a Catholic successor, but Shaftesbury argued that the only viable alternative to exclusion was calling on the king to remarry.[[2]](#cite_note-ODNB-3) On 23 December 1680, Shaftesbury gave another fiery pro-Exclusion speech in the Lords, in the course of which he attacked the Duke of York, expressed mistrust of Charles II, and urged the parliament to not approve any taxes until "the King shall satisfie the People, that what we give is not to make us Slaves and Papists".[[2]](#cite_note-ODNB-3) With parliament pursuing the Irish investigation vigorously, and threatening to impeach some of Charles II's judges, Charles prorogued parliament on 10 January 1681, and then dissolved it on 18 January, calling for fresh elections for a new parliament, to meet at [Oxford](/wiki/Oxford "Oxford") on 21 March 1681.[[2]](#cite_note-ODNB-3) On 25 January 1681, Shaftesbury, Essex, and Salisbury presented the king with a petition signed by sixteen peers asking that parliament should be held at [Westminster Hall](/wiki/Westminster_Hall "Westminster Hall") rather than Oxford, but the king remained committed to Oxford.[[2]](#cite_note-ODNB-3)

##### The Oxford Parliament, 1681

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Anthony_Ashley_Cooper,_1st_Earl_of_Shaftesbury&action=edit&section=17 "Edit section: The Oxford Parliament, 1681")]

In February 1681, Shaftesbury and his supporters brought another indictment against York, this time at the [Old Bailey](/wiki/Old_Bailey "Old Bailey"), with the grand jury this time finding the bill true, although York's counsel was able to pursue procedural delays until the prosecution lapsed.[[2]](#cite_note-ODNB-3)

At the [Oxford Parliament](/wiki/Oxford_Parliament_(1681) "Oxford Parliament (1681)"), Charles insisted he would listen to any reasonable expedient short of changing the line of succession that would assuage the nation's concerns about a Catholic successor.[[2]](#cite_note-ODNB-3) On 24 March 1681, Shaftesbury announced in the House of Lords that he had received an anonymous letter suggesting that the king's condition could be met if he were to declare the Duke of Monmouth legitimate.[[2]](#cite_note-ODNB-3) Charles was furious. On 26 March 1681, an Exclusion Bill was introduced in the Oxford Parliament and Charles dissolved parliament.[[2]](#cite_note-ODNB-3) The only issue the Oxford Parliament had resolved had been the case of [Edward Fitzharris](/wiki/Edward_Fitzharris "Edward Fitzharris"), who was to be left to the [common law](/wiki/Common_law "Common law"), although Shaftesbury and 19 other peers signed a formal protest of this result.[[2]](#cite_note-ODNB-3)

##### Prosecution for high treason, 1681–1682

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Anthony_Ashley_Cooper,_1st_Earl_of_Shaftesbury&action=edit&section=18 "Edit section: Prosecution for high treason, 1681–1682")]

The end of the Oxford Parliament marked the beginning of the so-called Tory Reaction.[[2]](#cite_note-ODNB-3) On 2 July 1681, Shaftesbury was arrested on suspicion of high treason and committed to the Tower of London. He immediately petitioned the Old Bailey on a [writ of habeas corpus](/wiki/Writ_of_habeas_corpus "Writ of habeas corpus"), but the Old Bailey said it did not have jurisdiction over prisoners in the Tower of London, so Shaftesbury had to wait for the next session of the [Court of King's Bench](/wiki/Court_of_King%27s_Bench_(England) "Court of King's Bench (England)").[[2]](#cite_note-ODNB-3) Shaftesbury moved for a writ of habeas corpus on 24 October 1681, and his case finally came before a grand jury on 24 November 1681.[[2]](#cite_note-ODNB-3)[[13]](#cite_note-14)

The government's case against Shaftesbury was particularly weak – most of the witnesses brought forth against Shaftesbury were witnesses whom the government admitted had already perjured themselves, and the documentary evidence was inconclusive.[[2]](#cite_note-ODNB-3) This, combined with the fact that the jury was handpicked by the Whig Sheriff of London, meant the government had little chance of securing a conviction and on 13 February 1682, the case against Shaftesbury was dropped.[[2]](#cite_note-ODNB-3) The announcement prompted great celebrations in London, with crowds yelling "No Popish Successor, No York, A Monmouth" and "God bless the Earl of Shaftesbury".[[2]](#cite_note-ODNB-3)

##### Attempts at an uprising, 1682

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Anthony_Ashley_Cooper,_1st_Earl_of_Shaftesbury&action=edit&section=19 "Edit section: Attempts at an uprising, 1682")]

In May 1682, Charles II fell ill, and Shaftesbury convened a group including Monmouth, Russell, [Ford Grey, 3rd Baron Grey of Werke](/wiki/Ford_Grey,_1st_Earl_of_Tankerville "Ford Grey, 1st Earl of Tankerville"), and [Sir Thomas Armstrong](/wiki/Sir_Thomas_Armstrong "Sir Thomas Armstrong") to determine what to do if the king died.[[2]](#cite_note-ODNB-3) They determined they would launch a rebellion demanding a parliament to settle the succession.[[2]](#cite_note-ODNB-3) The king recovered, however, and this was not necessary.[[2]](#cite_note-ODNB-3)

At the election of the Sheriffs of London in July 1682, the [Tory](/wiki/Tory "Tory") candidates prevailed.[[2]](#cite_note-ODNB-3) Shaftesbury was worried that these Sheriffs would be able to fill juries with Tory supporters and he was desperately afraid of another prosecution for high treason.[[2]](#cite_note-ODNB-3) Shaftesbury, therefore began discussions with Monmouth, Russell, and Grey to launch coordinated rebellions in different parts of the country.[[2]](#cite_note-ODNB-3) Shaftesbury was much more eager for a rebellion than the other three, and the uprising was postponed several times, to Shaftesbury's chagrin.[[2]](#cite_note-ODNB-3)

Following the installation of the new Tory sheriffs on 28 September 1682, Shaftesbury grew desperate.[[2]](#cite_note-ODNB-3) He continued to urge an immediate uprising and also opened discussions with [John Wildman](/wiki/John_Wildman "John Wildman") about the possibility of assassinating the king and the Duke of York.[[2]](#cite_note-ODNB-3)

##### Flight from England and death, 1682–1683

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Anthony_Ashley_Cooper,_1st_Earl_of_Shaftesbury&action=edit&section=20 "Edit section: Flight from England and death, 1682–1683")]

With his plots having proved unsuccessful, Shaftesbury determined to flee the country.[[2]](#cite_note-ODNB-3) He landed at [Brielle](/wiki/Brielle "Brielle") sometime between 20 and 26 November 1682, reached [Rotterdam](/wiki/Rotterdam "Rotterdam") on 28 November, and finally, arrived in [Amsterdam](/wiki/Amsterdam "Amsterdam") on 2 December 1682.[[2]](#cite_note-ODNB-3)

Shaftesbury's health had deteriorated markedly during this voyage. In Amsterdam, he fell ill, and by the end of December, he found it difficult to keep down any food.[[2]](#cite_note-ODNB-3) He drew up a [will](/wiki/Will_(law) "Will (law)") on 17 January 1683.[[2]](#cite_note-ODNB-3) On 20 January, in a conversation with [Robert Ferguson](/wiki/Robert_Ferguson_(minister) "Robert Ferguson (minister)"), who had accompanied him to Amsterdam, he professed himself an [Arian](/wiki/Arianism "Arianism").[[2]](#cite_note-ODNB-3) He died the next day, on 21 January 1683 at the residence of Abraham Kick, a Dutch merchant.[[14]](#cite_note-15)[[2]](#cite_note-ODNB-3)

According to the provisions of his will, Shaftesbury's body was shipped back to [Dorset](/wiki/Dorset "Dorset") on 13 February 1683, and he was buried at [Wimborne St Giles](/wiki/Wimborne_St_Giles "Wimborne St Giles") on 26 February 1683.[[2]](#cite_note-ODNB-3) Shaftesbury's son, [Lord Ashley](/wiki/Anthony_Ashley-Cooper,_2nd_Earl_of_Shaftesbury "Anthony Ashley-Cooper, 2nd Earl of Shaftesbury"), succeeded him as Earl of Shaftesbury.

## Legacy

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Anthony_Ashley_Cooper,_1st_Earl_of_Shaftesbury&action=edit&section=21 "Edit section: Legacy")]

|  |  |
| --- | --- |
| [[icon]](/wiki/File:Wiki_letter_w_cropped.svg) | This section **needs expansion**. You can help by [adding missing information](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Anthony_Ashley_Cooper,_1st_Earl_of_Shaftesbury&action=edit&section=). *(March 2022)* |

John Locke drafted an epitaph for Shaftesbury, calling him "a vigorous and indefatigable champion for civil and ecclesiastical liberty."[[15]](#cite_note-16) Although an early leader of the Whigs, "later generations disowned Shaftesbury," but they did uphold the principle he defended in Parliament's case for Exclusion, that "in the last resort the interests of the nation tak[e] precedence over those of the Crown."[[16]](#cite_note-17)

In North America, the [Cooper River](/wiki/Cooper_River_(South_Carolina) "Cooper River (South Carolina)")[[17]](#cite_note-18) and the [Ashley River](/wiki/Ashley_River_(South_Carolina) "Ashley River (South Carolina)") which merge in Charleston, South Carolina are named in his honour. The Ashley was given its current name by explorer [Robert Sandford](/wiki/Robert_Sandford_(explorer) "Robert Sandford (explorer)").[[18]](#cite_note-19)

In popular culture, Shaftesbury has been portrayed on screen by [Frederick Peisley](/wiki/Frederick_Peisley "Frederick Peisley") in *[The First Churchills](/wiki/The_First_Churchills "The First Churchills")* (1969), by [Martin Freeman](/wiki/Martin_Freeman "Martin Freeman") in *[Charles II: The Power and The Passion](/wiki/Charles_II:_The_Power_and_The_Passion "Charles II: The Power and The Passion")*, and by [Murray Melvin](/wiki/Murray_Melvin "Murray Melvin") in *[England, My England](/wiki/England,_My_England "England, My England")* (1995).
